Using a Smith machine for the upright row provides a single plane of vertical motion. A shoulder-width or narrower grip on the bar during the upright row emphasizes the trapezius. A wider grip on the bar during the upright row works the deltoid. Performing the upright row while standing vertically upright hits the trapezius centrally. Tilting your torso slightly back at the waist during the upright row targets the upper trapezius. Leaning slightly forward during the upright row targets the muscle lower down the neck. The higher the bar is raised during the upright row, the harder the trapezius works. Raising the bar higher during the upright row increases the risk of shoulder-impingement pain.
summary: The machine upright row targets the trapezius and posterior deltoid, with grip width and torso angle influencing muscle emphasis.

content: The primary muscles involved in the cable seated row are the middle and lower trapezius and latissimus dorsi. Secondary muscles involved in the cable seated row include the rhomboid major, rhomboid minor, and posterior deltoid. Spacing your hands farther apart during the cable seated row targets the outer trapezius. Placing your hands closer together during the cable seated row focuses on the inner portion of the trapezius. A pronated (overhand) grip during the cable seated row tends to target the upper and middle trapezius. A neutral grip (thumbs up) during the cable seated row hits the middle and lower trapezius. A supinated (underhand) grip during the cable seated row switches the focus to the latissimus dorsi. To target the trapezius during the cable seated row, pull the handles or bar through a high trajectory toward the chest. A low trajectory toward the abdomen during the cable seated row works the latissimus dorsi. Keep your back straight and your torso upright during the cable seated row.
summary: The cable seated row targets the trapezius and latissimus dorsi, with hand placement and grip influencing muscle emphasis.

content: The wide-grip pull-down targets the outer section of the latissimus dorsi. Secondary muscles involved in the wide-grip pull-down include the posterior deltoid, lower trapezius, rhomboid major, and rhomboid minor. For the wide-grip pull-down, an overhand grip is most effective.
summary: The wide-grip pull-down primarily targets the latissimus dorsi.

content: A low trajectory toward the abdomen during the cable seated row works the latissimus dorsi. Keep your back straight and your torso upright during the cable seated row.
summary: Cable seated row technique: maintain a straight back, and aim for a low trajectory towards the abdomen to target the latissimus dorsi.

content: The wide-grip pull-down targets the outer section of the latissimus dorsi. Secondary muscles involved in the wide-grip pull-down include the posterior deltoid, lower trapezius, rhomboid major, and rhomboid minor. For the wide-grip pull-down, an overhand grip is most effective. A wider hand spacing in the wide-grip pull-down emphasizes the outermost section of the latissimus dorsi under the armpit. Pulling the bar vertically downward using shoulder adduction emphasizes the outer latissimus dorsi.
summary: Wide-grip pull-down: targets the outer latissimus dorsi, using an overhand grip and wider hand spacing for maximum activation.

content: To maximize range of motion in the wide-grip pull-down, stretch the latissimus dorsi at the top position and squeeze it at the bottom of the movement. The angled ends of a wide-grip pull-down bar offer advantages over a straight bar, including improved trajectory, less stress through the wrist joint, and a few extra inches of motion before the bar touches the chest.
summary: Wide-grip pull-down technique: maximize range of motion by stretching at the top and squeezing at the bottom. Angled bars offer advantages over straight bars.

content: Pulling the bar down behind the neck in a pull-down exercise is a less favorable trajectory that can cause shoulder joint injury. Leaning the torso back about 30 degrees from vertical creates a trajectory that uses shoulder extension, emphasizing the inner lower latissimus.
summary: Pull-down trajectory: avoid pulling behind the neck, as it can cause injury. Lean back slightly to emphasize the inner lower latissimus using shoulder extension.

content: The close-grip pull-down targets the inner section of the latissimus dorsi. Secondary muscles involved in the close-grip pull-down include the biceps brachii, lower trapezius, and posterior deltoid. The primary muscle involved in the exercise is the inner section of the latissimus dorsi. Secondary muscles involved in the exercise include the lower trapezius, rhomboid major, rhomboid minor, posterior deltoid, and biceps brachii.
summary: Close-grip pull-down: targets the inner latissimus dorsi, with secondary involvement from biceps, lower trapezius, and posterior deltoid.

content: As hand spacing narrows, the focus shifts to the innermost section of the latissimus dorsi, generating thickness and depth in the middle back.
summary: Close-grip pull-down: narrower hand spacing emphasizes the innermost latissimus dorsi, building thickness and depth in the middle back.

content: The pull-up is similar to the pull-down except that the resistance provided by your own body weight is not easily adjusted. Resistance may be added using a weighted belt.
summary: Pull-up vs. pull-down: similar movement, but bodyweight resistance is fixed. Add weight with a belt for increased challenge.

content: Take an overhand grip on the pull-up bar with hands 6 inches (15 cm) wider than shoulder width and arms extended. Pull your torso upward until your chin touches the bar. Lower your torso slowly down to the starting position.
summary: Pull-up technique: overhand grip, wider than shoulder width, pull chin to bar, and slowly lower back down.

content: The primary muscle involved in the wide-grip pull-up is the outer section of the latissimus dorsi. Secondary muscles involved in the wide-grip pull-up include the posterior deltoid, lower trapezius, and rhomboid major.
summary: Wide-grip pull-up: primarily targets the outer latissimus dorsi, with secondary activation of posterior deltoid, lower trapezius, and rhomboid major.

content: As the hand spacing gets wider, the focus shifts to the outermost section of the latissimus dorsi at the armpit. This portion of the muscle creates width across the back. An overhand (pronated) grip works best for this exercise.
summary: Wide-grip pull-up: wider hand spacing emphasizes the outermost latissimus dorsi, creating width. Overhand grip is most effective.

content: Because the torso remains vertical during the movement, pull-ups primarily use shoulder adduction and therefore tend to work the outer latissimus dorsi. To maximize range of motion, stretch the latissimus dorsi at the bottom position and squeeze the latissimus dorsi at the top of the movement by pulling the elbows down and back.
summary: Pull-up movement: torso remains vertical, emphasizing shoulder adduction and outer latissimus activation. Maximize range of motion with stretches at the bottom and squeezes at the top.

content: Crossing one foot over the other and slightly bending the knees minimizes body swing during the movement.
summary: Pull-up technique: crossing feet and slightly bending knees helps minimize body swing.

content: An underhand (supinated) grip on the bar facilitates a closer hand spacing, emphasizing shoulder extension rather than adduction. As the hand spacing gets narrower, the focus shifts to target the inner lower section of the latissimus dorsi.
summary: Underhand grip pull-up: closer hand spacing emphasizes shoulder extension and targets the inner lower latissimus dorsi.

content: Handlebar attachments allow a neutral grip (palms facing together). An overhand grip targets the outer latissimus dorsi, an underhand grip isolates the inner latissimus dorsi, and a neutral grip hits the muscle centrally.
summary: Pull-up grip variations: overhand targets outer latissimus, underhand targets inner, and neutral grip hits the muscle centrally.

content: Because the torso remains vertical during the movement, pull-ups primarily use shoulder adduction and therefore tend to work the outer latissimus dorsi. To maximize range of motion, stretch the latissimus dorsi at the bottom position and squeeze the latissimus dorsi at the top of the movement by pulling the elbows down and back. Crossing one foot over the other and slightly bending the knees minimizes body swing during the movement. Pulling up with the back of your neck touching the bar is a less favorable trajectory that may cause irritation to the shoulder joint.
summary: Pull-ups primarily target the outer latissimus dorsi, with technique tips for maximizing range of motion and minimizing injury.

content: An underhand (supinated) grip on the bar facilitates a closer hand spacing, emphasizing shoulder extension rather than adduction. As the hand spacing gets narrower, the focus shifts to target the inner lower section of the latissimus dorsi. With an underhand grip, the greater contribution from the biceps brachii provides added strength.
summary: Underhand grip variations in pull-ups emphasize shoulder extension and target different sections of the latissimus dorsi.

content: The handlebar attachment on some equipment allows a neutral grip with palms facing each other. Whereas a wide overhand grip targets the outer latissimus dorsi and a close underhand grip isolates the inner latissimus dorsi, a neutral grip hits the muscle centrally.
summary: Different grip variations on pull-up equipment target different sections of the latissimus dorsi.

content: The barbell row targets the latissimus dorsi, erector spinae (iliocostalis, longissimus, spinalis), trapezius, rhomboid major, rhomboid minor, and posterior deltoid. A typical barbell row execution involves taking an overhand shoulder-width grip on the barbell with arms extended, bending the torso forward 45 degrees to the floor, pulling the bar vertically upward to touch the lower chest while keeping the spine straight and knees slightly bent, and lowering the bar down to the starting position. Hand spacing during a barbell row influences muscle activation; a shoulder-width or closer spacing targets the central inner section of the latissimus dorsi, while a wider grip targets the outer latissimus dorsi. An underhand grip on the barbell during a row facilitates a closer hand spacing, emphasizing shoulder extension and targeting the central inner section of the latissimus dorsi. An underhand grip on the barbell during a row increases the contribution of the biceps brachii, providing added strength during the movement.
summary: The barbell row targets multiple muscle groups, with hand spacing and grip influencing muscle activation.

content: Pulling the barbell upward higher toward the chest during a row targets the upper latissimus dorsi and trapezius. Pulling the barbell through a lower trajectory to touch the abdomen during a row targets the lower latissimus dorsi. Maintaining a straight spine during a barbell row is crucial; rounding the lower back can lead to injury.
summary: Barbell row technique variations target different sections of the latissimus dorsi and emphasize proper form.

content: The T-bar row variation requires less effort to stabilize body position because one end of the bar pivots at a fixed point on the floor. In the T-bar row, stand facing the loaded end with feet positioned on either side of the bar, keeping the spine straight and knees slightly bent, and lift the loaded end using the T-bar attachment. Some T-bar row apparatuses feature an inclined chest pad that supports the torso and minimizes load across the lower spine.
summary: The T-bar row is a variation that reduces stabilization demands and offers support options.

content: The dumbbell row targets the latissimus dorsi, trapezius, rhomboid major, biceps brachii, and middle back. A typical dumbbell row execution involves grasping a dumbbell in one hand with the palm facing in, resting the opposite hand and knee on a bench while keeping the spine straight and just above parallel to the floor, pulling the dumbbell vertically upward alongside the torso, raising the elbow as high as possible, and lowering the dumbbell down to the starting position.
summary: The dumbbell row targets multiple muscle groups, with proper form emphasizing a straight spine.

content: The middle back exercise targets the latissimus dorsi. Secondary muscles involved in the middle back exercise include the trapezius, rhomboid major, rhomboid minor, posterior deltoid, and erector spinae (iliocostalis, longissimus, spinalis) and biceps brachii.
summary: The middle back exercise primarily targets the latissimus dorsi with secondary activation of other muscle groups.

content: A neutral grip with the dumbbell parallel to the torso works best for the one-arm dumbbell row. Pulling the dumbbell toward the chest works the upper latissimus dorsi and lower trapezius. Raising the dumbbell through a lower trajectory toward the abdomen targets the lower latissimus dorsi. Maximize the range of motion by stretching the latissimus dorsi at the bottom position and raising the elbow as high as possible at the top of the movement. Supporting your torso on the bench reduces stress through the spine.
summary: One-arm dumbbell row technique variations target different sections of the latissimus dorsi and emphasize proper form.

content: Grab the handle of a low pulley with one hand using a neutral (thumb-up) grip for the one-arm cable seated row. Pull the handle high toward your chest, keeping your spine straight for the one-arm cable seated row. Return the handle to the starting position with the arm extended for the one-arm cable seated row. Rowing one arm at a time allows you to pull the elbow back farther, thereby maximizing muscle contraction in the latissimus dorsi.
summary: The one-arm cable seated row targets the latissimus dorsi with a focus on maximizing muscle contraction.

content: Grab the handles of the machine with your arms extended in front of your body for the machine row. Support your torso against the chest pad if the machine has one for the machine row. Pull the handles toward your upper abdomen, keeping your spine straight for the machine row. Return the weight to the starting position for the machine row. Spacing your hands farther apart targets the outer latissimus dorsi. Spacing your hands closer together isolates the inner latissimus dorsi. A pronated (overhand) grip tends to target the upper and outer latissimus dorsi.
summary: The machine row allows for hand spacing and grip variations to target different sections of the latissimus dorsi.

content: Pull the handles toward your upper abdomen, keeping your spine straight for the machine row. Return the weight to the starting position for the machine row.
summary: Instructions for performing a machine row exercise.

content: Spacing your hands farther apart targets the outer latissimus dorsi. Spacing your hands closer together isolates the inner latissimus dorsi. A pronated (overhand) grip tends to target the upper and outer latissimus dorsi. A neutral (thumbs up) grip hits the central section of the back. A supinated (underhand) grip works the lower latissimus dorsi. As the grip changes from pronation to neutral to supination, the elbows move progressively closer to the sides of your body.
summary: How hand placement and grip affect muscle activation during the machine row.

content: Pulling the handles through a high trajectory toward the chest targets the upper latissimus dorsi and trapezius. A lower trajectory toward the abdomen targets the lower latissimus dorsi. Adjust the seat height to change trajectory. Raising the seat creates a low trajectory; lowering the seat provides a high trajectory.
summary: How trajectory affects muscle activation during the machine row.

content: Pull your elbows back as far as possible and squeeze your shoulder blades together to maximize muscle contraction. Load across the spine is reduced when the torso is supported against a chest pad.
summary: Technique tips for maximizing muscle contraction during the machine row.

content: The lumbar extension exercise involves lying facedown with hips supported on a bench and ankles secured under pads. Begin the lumbar extension with your torso hanging down, bent 90 degrees at the waist. Raise your upper body during the lumbar extension until your torso is just above parallel to the floor.
summary: Instructions for performing the lumbar extension exercise.

content: Primary muscles involved in the lumbar extension are the erector spinae (iliocostalis, longissimus, spinalis). Secondary muscles involved in the lumbar extension are the latissimus dorsi, gluteus maximus, and hamstrings (semitendinosus, semimembranosus, biceps femoris.)
summary: Primary and secondary muscles involved in the lumbar extension.

content: Hand position for the lumbar extension can be interlocked behind the lower back or folded across the chest. Resistance can be added to the lumbar extension by holding a weight plate against the front of the chest.
summary: Hand positions and resistance options for the lumbar extension.

content: The lumbar extension movement should occur through an arc of about 90 degrees. Avoid hyperextending your spine during the lumbar extension. The erector spinae muscles stabilize and straighten the spine during the lumbar extension. The gluteus maximus and hamstrings generate hip extension during the lumbar extension.
summary: Movement parameters and muscle actions during the lumbar extension.

content: The incline lumbar extension is easier because the hips are supported high and the ankles are closer to the floor. The incline lumbar extension shifts the focus away from the lumbar muscles onto the buttocks and hamstrings.
summary: Comparison of incline lumbar extension to standard lumbar extension.

content: The machine lumbar extension is performed while seated on a machine that provides variable resistance. Avoid flexing the spine too far forward or extending too far backward during the machine lumbar extension.
summary: Description of the machine lumbar extension exercise.

content: The deadlift involves taking a shoulder-width overhand grip on a barbell with arms extended. Squat down, bending the knees and hips during the deadlift. Keep your spine straight and elbows stiff while standing upright and lifting the bar upward to hip level during the deadlift. Slowly lower the bar back to the floor by bending at the knees and hips during the deadlift.
summary: Instructions for performing the barbell deadlift.

content: The primary muscles involved in the barbell deadlift are the erector spinae (iliocostalis, longissimus, spinalis), gluteus maximus, and hamstrings (semitendinosus, semimembranosus, biceps femoris). Secondary muscles involved in the barbell deadlift include the trapezius, latissimus dorsi, quadriceps (rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, vastus intermedius), and forearms (wrist flexors, finger flexors).
summary: Primary and secondary muscles involved in the barbell deadlift.

content: For the barbell deadlift, hands should be spaced shoulder-width apart so that the arms hang vertically and the hands pass along the outer thighs. An over-under grip with one palm facing forward and the other facing back is recommended for the barbell deadlift to prevent the bar from rolling. Feet should be positioned directly below the hips with toes pointing straight ahead for the barbell deadlift.
summary: Hand and foot placement for the barbell deadlift.

content: The barbell should travel straight up and down and stay close to the body during the barbell deadlift. The range of motion for the barbell deadlift is from the floor to the tops of the thighs, keeping the arms extended and elbows stiff.
summary: Barbell movement and range of motion during the barbell deadlift.

content: For the barbell deadlift, hands should be spaced shoulder-width apart so that the arms hang vertically and the hands pass along the outer thighs. An over-under grip with one palm facing forward and the other facing back is recommended for the barbell deadlift to prevent the bar from rolling. Feet should be positioned directly below the hips with toes pointing straight ahead for the barbell deadlift. The barbell should travel straight up and down and stay close to the body during the barbell deadlift. The range of motion for the barbell deadlift is from the floor to the tops of the thighs, keeping the arms extended and elbows stiff. During the barbell deadlift, the erector spinae muscles stabilize and straighten the spine while the gluteus maximus and hamstrings generate hip extension. The spine should be kept straight throughout the barbell deadlift movement.
summary: Barbell deadlift technique: grip, stance, movement, and muscle engagement.

content: The barbell stiff-legged deadlift shifts the focus from the lower back to the buttocks and hamstrings. The sumo-style deadlift, performed with a stance wider than hip-width, emphasizes the thigh muscles.
summary: Variations of the deadlift: stiff-legged and sumo.

content: For the machine deadlift, stand upright and hold the bars of a Smith machine at arms' length using an overhand shoulder-width grip. Bend forward at the waist and lower the bar downward, keeping the spine straight and elbows stiff during the machine deadlift. Lift the bar back up to the starting position to complete the machine deadlift. A Smith machine provides a single plane of vertical motion that can help focus effort during the machine deadlift. An overhand grip is used for the machine deadlift to maintain the Smith machine bar in the unlocked position. A slight bend in the knees helps with execution during the machine deadlift. The range of motion for the machine deadlift is from hip level to down beyond the knees and back up again, keeping the arms extended and elbows stiff. During the machine deadlift, the erector spinae muscles stabilize and straighten the spine while the gluteus maximus and hamstrings generate hip extension.
summary: Machine deadlift technique and benefits.

content: The cable pull-through is a variation of the deadlift performed facing away from a low pulley using a short bar with the cable passing between the legs.
summary: Cable pull-through: a deadlift variation.

content: The Good Morning exercise targets the lower back. The Good Morning exercise involves bending forward at the waist until the torso is just above parallel to the floor. The Good Morning exercise requires keeping the spine straight and knees stiff (straight or slightly bent). The primary muscles involved in the Good Morning exercise are the erector spinae (iliocostalis, longissimus, spinalis). Secondary muscles involved in the Good Morning exercise include the latissimus dorsi, gluteus maximus, and hamstrings (semitendinosus, semimembranosus, biceps femoris). The grip for the Good Morning exercise should be slightly wider than shoulder-width apart using an overhand grip. A slight bend in the knees helps with the execution of the Good Morning exercise. The range of motion for the Good Morning exercise is about 90 degrees. The erector spinae muscles stabilize and straighten the spine during the Good Morning exercise. The gluteus maximus and hamstrings generate hip extension during the Good Morning exercise. A variation of the Good Morning exercise is the machine lift, which is performed while seated using a pad across the upper back to provide resistance.
summary: The Good Morning exercise: technique, muscles targeted, and variations.

content: The human arm is divided into the upper arm and lower arm (forearm). The upper arm consists of one bone, the humerus, while the forearm consists of two bones, the radius (located on the thumb side) and ulna (located on the little-finger side). The elbow is a hinge joint formed at the junction of the humerus, radius, and ulna. Two movements occur at the elbow joint: flexion and extension. During elbow flexion, the forearm moves toward the upper arm. During elbow extension, the forearm moves away from the upper arm. Movement also takes place in the forearm when the radius rotates around the ulna. Supination (palm up) and pronation (palm down) take place between the radioulnar joints. The wrist joint is the junction between the lower end of the forearm bones and the small bones in the hand.
summary: Anatomy of the human arm: bones, joints, and movements.

content: The biceps brachii muscle has two heads: the short head attaches to the coracoid process, and the long head arises from above the glenoid of the shoulder joint. The biceps brachii muscle passes down alongside the humerus and attaches about 1.5 inches (4 cm) below the elbow joint onto a tuberosity on the inside of the radius bone.
summary: Biceps brachii muscle: origin, insertion, and function.

content: The wrist joint is the junction between the lower end of the forearm bones and the small bones in the hand. Movement also takes place in the forearm when the radius rotates around the ulna. Supination (palm up) and pronation (palm down) take place between the radioulnar joints.
summary: The wrist joint connects the forearm to the hand, and forearm movement involves the radius rotating around the ulna, leading to supination and pronation.

content: The biceps brachii muscle has two heads: the short head attaches to the coracoid process, and the long head arises from above the glenoid of the shoulder joint. The biceps brachii passes down alongside the humerus and attaches about 1.5 inches (4 cm) below the elbow joint onto a tuberosity on the inside of the radius bone. The biceps brachii causes flexion at the elbow joint, such as when raising the hand toward the face. The biceps brachii also causes supination of the forearm, such as when rotating the hand so that the palm faces up.
summary: The biceps brachii muscle, with its short and long heads, originates at the shoulder and attaches to the radius, responsible for elbow flexion and forearm supination.

content: Two other muscles that flex (bend) the elbow are the brachialis and brachioradialis. The brachialis muscle lies deep beneath the biceps brachii, arising from the lower half of the humerus and attaching to the ulna bone just below the elbow joint. The brachialis lifts the ulna at the same time that the biceps brachii lifts the radius.
summary: The brachialis and brachioradialis muscles, along with the biceps brachii, contribute to elbow flexion.

content: The brachioradialis muscle arises from the outer aspect of the lower end of the humerus and then travels down the forearm to attach to the radius just above the wrist joint.
summary: The brachioradialis muscle originates from the humerus and attaches to the radius, contributing to elbow flexion.

content: The triceps brachii muscle has three heads: the long head arises from beneath the glenoid fossa of the shoulder joint, the lateral (outer) head arises from the outer surface of the humerus, and the medial (inner) head arises from the medial and rear surfaces of the humerus. All three heads of the triceps brachii fuse at their lower ends to form a single tendon that attaches behind the elbow joint onto the olecranon process of the ulna bone. The triceps brachii causes extension at the elbow, such as when straightening the arm.
summary: The triceps brachii muscle, composed of three heads, originates from various points on the humerus and attaches to the ulna, responsible for elbow extension.

content: The forearm is a mass of 20 muscles. The forearm has two separate muscle compartments: the flexor group on the palm side and the extensor group on the reverse side. The fleshy muscle portions of almost all forearm muscles are located in the upper two-thirds of the forearm. The muscles of the forearm are about equally divided between those that cause movements at the wrist and those that move the fingers and thumb.
summary: The forearm contains 20 muscles divided into flexor and extensor groups, primarily located in the upper two-thirds, with functions at the wrist and fingers.

content: The wrist flexors and extensors are superficial (under the skin), whereas the finger flexors and extensors are deep (close to the bone). The superficial group of wrist flexors and extensors cross both the wrist and elbow joints, so therefore have a greater stretch when wrist curls are performed with the elbow joint straight.
summary: Forearm muscles are categorized as superficial (wrist) or deep (finger), with wrist muscles having greater stretch during wrist curls with straight elbows.

content: The wrist flexors are the palmaris longus, flexor carpi radialis, and flexor carpi ulnaris. The wrist extensors are extensor carpi radialis longus, extensor carpi radialis brevis, and extensor carpi ulnaris.
summary: The wrist flexors include palmaris longus, flexor carpi radialis, and flexor carpi ulnaris, while the wrist extensors are extensor carpi radialis longus, brevis, and ulnaris.

content: The finger flexors are the flexor digitorum superficialis, flexor digitorum profundus, and flexor pollicis longus. The finger extensors are the extensor digitorum, extensor pollicis longus, extensor pollicis brevis, and extensor indicis.
summary: Finger flexors include flexor digitorum superficialis, profundus, and pollicis longus, while finger extensors are extensor digitorum, pollicis longus, brevis, and indicis.

content: Supination, or rotating the hand so the palm faces up, is performed by the supinator and biceps brachii muscles. Pronation, or rotating the hand so the palm faces down, is performed by the pronator teres and pronator quadratus.
summary: Supination (palm up) is performed by the supinator and biceps brachii, while pronation (palm down) is performed by the pronator teres and quadratus.

content: A wide grip during a biceps curl focuses effort on the inner biceps brachii (short head). A narrow grip during a biceps curl works the outer biceps brachii (long head). With a straight bar, the underhand grip is fixed in supination (palms facing up) during a biceps curl. The grip may be adjusted using an EZ bar during a biceps curl.
summary: Biceps curl grip variations (wide, narrow) target different biceps heads, and the EZ bar allows for a less supinated grip.

content: The bar should move up and down in an arc close to the body during a biceps curl. To isolate the biceps brachii during a biceps curl, motion should occur at the elbows and not the shoulders. Stopping a few degrees short of full elbow extension keeps tension on the biceps brachii as the barbell is lowered during a biceps curl.
summary: Proper biceps curl technique involves an arc motion close to the body, isolating the elbows, and stopping short of full extension to maintain tension.

content: Stand upright with the spine straight during a biceps curl. Tilting the torso during a biceps curl cheats the bar upward with momentum. Leaning slightly forward during a biceps curl makes the initial phase of the curl easier. Leaning slightly backward during a biceps curl helps complete the final phase of the repetition.
summary: Proper biceps curl posture involves a straight spine, avoiding momentum, and slight leaning adjustments for easier initial and final phases.

content: Performing a curl with an EZ bar changes the grip from fully supinated (palms facing up) to a less supinated, nearly neutral (palms facing in) grip. The EZ bar grip emphasizes the outer (long) head of the biceps brachii, the brachialis, and is less strenuous on the wrist joints.
summary: The EZ bar grip alters the hand position, emphasizing the long head of the biceps brachii and brachialis while reducing wrist strain.

content: To perform a dumbbell curl, sit on the edge of a weight bench with both feet planted on the floor.
summary: Dumbbell curls are performed sitting on a bench with feet planted on the floor.

content: Leaning slightly forward during a biceps curl makes the initial phase of the curl easier. Leaning slightly backward during a biceps curl helps complete the final phase of the repetition.
summary: Adjusting body lean during a biceps curl can aid in overcoming the initial and final phases of the repetition.

content: Performing a curl with an EZ bar changes the grip from fully supinated (palms facing up) to a less supinated, nearly neutral (palms facing in) grip. The EZ bar grip emphasizes the outer (long) head of the biceps brachii, the brachialis, and is less strenuous on the wrist joints.
summary: The EZ bar grip modifies the curl, emphasizing the outer biceps head and reducing wrist strain.

content: To perform a dumbbell curl, sit on the edge of a weight bench with both feet planted on the floor. Hold a dumbbell in each hand at arms’ length by your sides, thumbs pointing forward, to begin a dumbbell curl. Curl one dumbbell up toward your shoulder, rotating your hand so your palm faces upward, during a dumbbell curl. Lower the dumbbell back down to the starting position and repeat with the opposite arm during a dumbbell curl.
summary: The dumbbell curl involves a seated position, starting with dumbbells at arm's length and curling upwards, rotating the hand to supinate.

content: The primary muscle involved in a dumbbell curl is the biceps brachii. Secondary muscles involved in a dumbbell curl include the brachialis, brachioradialis, anterior deltoid, and forearm muscles (wrist flexors, finger flexors). The dumbbell curl works the biceps brachii in two ways: elbow flexion and forearm supination. To maximize contraction of the biceps brachii during a dumbbell curl, supinate the hand (palm facing up) as the dumbbell is raised. Slide your palm over so your thumb rests against the inside of the plate when performing a dumbbell curl to increase the load on the biceps brachii during supination.
summary: The dumbbell curl primarily targets the biceps brachii, with secondary muscle activation and emphasis on elbow flexion and forearm supination.

content: Position your torso upright with your spine straight during a dumbbell curl. Tilting the torso during a dumbbell curl cheats the weight upward with momentum. Use a full range of motion at the elbow during a dumbbell curl.
summary: Proper form for dumbbell curls includes an upright torso, avoiding momentum, and using a full range of motion.

content: The dumbbell standing curl can be performed in a standing position, but this requires muscular effort in the legs. The seated version of the dumbbell curl affords better focus.
summary: Dumbbell curls can be performed standing or seated, with the seated version offering better focus on the biceps.

content: Performing the dumbbell incline curl while seated on an incline bench helps focus effort on the lower portion of the biceps brachii, near the elbow.
summary: The dumbbell incline curl targets the lower portion of the biceps brachii.

content: The Concentration Curl is an exercise for the biceps brachii. To perform the Concentration Curl, sit on the edge of a bench and hold a dumbbell in one hand at arm’s length, supporting your arm against the inside of your thigh. Place your free hand on your other thigh. Curl the dumbbell up toward your shoulder by bending at the elbow. Lower the dumbbell back down to the starting position. The primary muscle involved in the Concentration Curl is the biceps brachii. Secondary muscles involved in the Concentration Curl include the brachialis, brachioradialis, forearm muscles (wrist flexors, finger flexors). An underhand grip maximizes contraction of the biceps brachii.
summary: The Concentration Curl is a biceps exercise that isolates the muscle by resting the upper arm against the thigh.

content: When the arm is vertical (shoulder directly above the elbow), resistance increases as the dumbbell is raised and effort is focused on the upper biceps brachii (peak). When the arm is at an inclined angle (elbow in front of the shoulder), resistance is maximal at the start, so effort targets the lower section of the biceps brachii at the elbow.
summary: The angle of the arm during a curl influences the targeted section of the biceps brachii.

content: Resting the upper arm against the thigh prevents movement at the shoulder and isolates the biceps brachii. The torso should remain motionless during the Concentration Curl.
summary: Maintaining a stable torso and resting the upper arm prevents shoulder movement, isolating the biceps brachii.

content: The Cable Curl is an exercise for the biceps brachii. To perform the Cable Curl, face the weight stack and grasp the short bar attached to a low pulley using an underhand grip with arms straight. Curl the bar up toward your shoulders by bending at the elbows. Lower the bar back down to the starting position. The primary muscle involved in the Cable Curl is the biceps brachii. Secondary muscles involved in the Cable Curl include the brachialis, brachioradialis, anterior deltoid, forearm muscles (wrist flexors, finger flexors).
summary: The Cable Curl is a biceps exercise using a cable machine, targeting the primary muscle and secondary muscle groups.

content: A grip that is wider than shoulder width during a biceps exercise focuses effort on the inner biceps brachii (short head). A narrow grip during a biceps exercise works the outer biceps brachii (long head).
summary: Grip width during a biceps exercise influences which head of the biceps brachii is primarily targeted.

content: The primary muscle involved in the Cable Curl is the biceps brachii. Secondary muscles involved in the Cable Curl include the brachialis, brachioradialis, anterior deltoid, forearm muscles (wrist flexors, finger flexors).
summary: The Cable Curl primarily targets the biceps brachii, with secondary activation of the brachialis, brachioradialis, anterior deltoid, and forearm muscles.

content: A grip that is wider than shoulder width during a biceps exercise focuses effort on the inner biceps brachii (short head). A narrow grip during a biceps exercise works the outer biceps brachii (long head).

content: With a straight bar, the underhand grip is fixed in supination (palms facing up) during a biceps exercise. Using an EZ bar during a biceps exercise switches the grip from the fully supinated position to a less supinated, nearly neutral (palms facing in) grip.
summary: Grip variations in biceps exercises include straight bars with supinated grips and EZ bars with less supinated grips, each influencing muscle activation.

content: The EZ bar grip during a biceps exercise tends to focus effort on the outer (long) head of the biceps brachii and the brachialis muscle. The EZ bar grip during a biceps exercise is less strenuous on the wrist joints.
summary: The EZ bar grip in biceps exercises emphasizes the outer biceps head and brachialis, while being gentler on the wrists compared to straight bars.

content: Stand upright with a straight spine during a biceps exercise. Fixing your elbows against your sides during a biceps exercise prevents movement at the shoulder and isolates the biceps brachii.
summary: Proper form in biceps exercises involves standing upright, maintaining a straight spine, and fixing elbows to isolate the biceps muscle.

content: Unlike barbell or dumbbell curls, the cable pulley provides uniform resistance throughout the movement during a biceps exercise.
summary: Cable curls offer consistent resistance throughout the movement, unlike barbell or dumbbell curls, which have varying resistance.

content: To perform a high-pulley curl, stand midway between the pulleys on a cable machine, grasp the D-handles attached to two high pulleys using an underhand grip, and hold your arms at shoulder level. During a high-pulley curl, curl the handles toward your head.
summary: The high-pulley curl is performed by standing midway between cable pulleys, using an underhand grip, and curling the handles upward, emphasizing the long head of the biceps.

content: To perform a one-arm cable curl, use a D-handle attached to the low pulley of a cable machine and perform the exercise with one arm at a time.
summary: The one-arm cable curl isolates each arm, allowing for focused training and improved muscle activation.

content: To perform a barbell preacher curl, sit with your upper arms resting on the preacher bench, take a shoulder-width underhand grip on the bar with your arms out straight, curl the bar up toward your shoulders, and lower the weight back down to the starting position.
summary: The barbell preacher curl is performed on a preacher bench, isolating the biceps and emphasizing the lower portion of the muscle.

content: The dumbbell preacher curl targets the biceps brachii, brachialis, brachioradialis, and forearm muscles (wrist flexors, finger flexors). The dumbbell preacher curl is performed by sitting with your upper arm resting on a preacher bench, holding a dumbbell in one hand at arm’s length, curling the dumbbell up toward your shoulder by bending at the elbow, and lowering the dumbbell back down to the starting position.
summary: The dumbbell preacher curl is a variation of the preacher curl, targeting the biceps, brachialis, brachioradialis, and forearm muscles.

content: Performing the dumbbell preacher curl one arm at a time with a dumbbell improves focus and isolation. An underhand (palm facing up) grip during the dumbbell preacher curl maximizes contraction of the biceps brachii.
summary: Performing dumbbell preacher curls one arm at a time and using an underhand grip enhances focus, isolation, and biceps contraction.

content: When the upper arm is supported at an inclined angle during the dumbbell preacher curl, resistance is maximal at the start, so effort is targeted on the lower section of the biceps brachii near the elbow. Resting the upper arm on the bench during the dumbbell preacher curl prevents movement at the shoulder and thereby helps isolate the biceps brachii.
summary: The angled support in the dumbbell preacher curl maximizes resistance at the start, targeting the lower biceps, while resting the upper arm isolates the muscle.

content: Stopping a few degrees short of full elbow extension during the dumbbell preacher curl keeps tension on the biceps brachii as the weight is lowered.
summary: Stopping short of full elbow extension during the dumbbell preacher curl maintains tension on the biceps throughout the movement.

content: Adjust the seat height so that your armpit is snug against the upper edge of the pad during the dumbbell preacher curl.
summary: Proper seat height adjustment in the dumbbell preacher curl ensures optimal support and muscle isolation.

summary: The machine curl primarily targets the biceps brachii, brachioradialis, and brachialis muscles.

content: The triceps push-down exercise primarily targets the triceps brachii muscle. Secondary muscles involved in the triceps push-down exercise include the deltoid and forearm muscles (wrist extensors). A wide grip during a triceps push-down exercise focuses effort on the inner triceps brachii (long head), while a narrow grip focuses effort on the outer triceps brachii (lateral head).
summary: The triceps push-down exercise primarily targets the triceps brachii, with grip width influencing muscle emphasis.

content: A pronated (palms down) grip during a triceps push-down exercise emphasizes the outer lateral head of the triceps brachii, while a supinated (palms up) grip focuses effort on the inner long head. A V-shaped bar during a triceps push-down exercise uses a neutral (thumbs up) grip that equally targets all three heads of the triceps brachii.
summary: Grip variations in the triceps push-down exercise target different triceps heads.

content: When the upper arms are perpendicular to the floor during a triceps push-down exercise, the outer triceps brachii (lateral head) contributes to the movement. Performing a triceps push-down exercise with arms raised parallel to the floor focuses effort on the inner triceps brachii (long head). Fixing the upper arms against your sides during a triceps push-down exercise prevents movement at the shoulders and isolates the triceps brachii. Motion during a triceps push-down exercise should occur through the elbow only.
summary: Triceps push-down exercise technique influences muscle activation and isolation.

content: Unlike barbell or dumbbell exercises, a cable provides uniform resistance throughout the movement during a triceps push-down exercise. Standing upright with a straight spine is the standard position for a triceps push-down exercise. Leaning the torso slightly forward at the waist during a triceps push-down exercise provides better stability when using heavier weights.
summary: Triceps push-down exercise technique and equipment considerations.

content: The rope attachment during a rope push-down exercise affords a forcible pronation at the wrist, targeting the outer lateral head of the triceps brachii. A reverse (underhand) grip during a reverse-grip push-down exercise focuses effort on the inner long head of the triceps brachii. Performing a one-arm push-down exercise with a D-handle, using either an overhand or underhand grip, focuses effort and improves isolation.
summary: Triceps push-down exercise variations target specific triceps heads and improve isolation.

content: The dip targets the triceps brachii. The primary muscles involved in the dip are the triceps brachii, pectoralis major, anterior deltoid, forearm muscles (wrist extensors, finger extensors). A wide grip during a dip focuses effort on the inner triceps brachii (long head). A narrow grip during a dip focuses effort on the outer triceps brachii (lateral head).
summary: The dip primarily targets the triceps brachii, with grip width influencing muscle emphasis.

content: The standard grip (palms facing each other and thumbs forward) during a dip hits all three heads of the triceps brachii and emphasizes the inner long head. Reversing the grip during a dip so that the palms face outward and the thumbs point back switches most of the effort to the outer triceps brachii (long head). Keeping your elbows close to your sides during a dip helps isolate the triceps brachii. Flaring your elbows out wide during a dip allows the chest muscles to assist.
summary: Dip grip variations and elbow position influence muscle activation and isolation.

content: To isolate the triceps brachii during a dip, keep motion at the shoulders to a minimum. To focus effort on the triceps brachii during a dip, keep your body upright. Leaning forward during a dip makes the chest muscles do more work. Resistance in a dip is provided by your body weight and is not easily adjusted. You can add resistance to a dip by attaching a weighted belt around your hips. Performing a dip in a triceps push-down machine makes it easier to focus your effort on the triceps brachii.
summary: Dip technique and resistance considerations.

content: To focus effort on the triceps brachii during a dip, keep your body upright. Leaning forward during a dip makes the chest muscles do more work. Performing a dip in a triceps push-down machine makes it easier to focus your effort on the triceps brachii. The tips mentioned for the dip also apply to the machine variation of the dip.
summary: This chunk describes how to perform dips effectively, emphasizing triceps engagement and adjusting resistance.

content: The lying triceps extension targets the triceps brachii. The primary muscles involved in the lying triceps extension are the triceps brachii, pectoralis major, deltoid, forearm muscles (wrist extensors, finger extensors). In the lying triceps extension, hold a barbell or EZ bar at arms’ length above your chest with an overhand grip, hands spaced approximately 6 inches (15 cm) apart.
summary: This chunk details the lying triceps extension exercise, its target muscles, and proper form.

content: The triceps brachii muscle has three heads: the long head, the lateral head, and the medial head. A wide grip during a triceps exercise emphasizes the inner triceps brachii (long head). A narrow grip during a triceps exercise targets the outer triceps brachii (lateral head). The vertical position of the arm during a triceps exercise stretches the inner (long) head of the triceps brachii. Lowering the bar beyond the forehead toward the bench during a triceps exercise generates a greater stretch in the long head, favoring its contraction during the movement.
summary: This chunk explains the anatomy of the triceps brachii and how grip width and arm position affect muscle activation.

content: To isolate the triceps brachii during an exercise, motion should occur only at the elbows, not at the shoulders. An overhand grip on a straight bar during a triceps exercise works the inner (long) head of the triceps brachii. An underhand grip on a straight bar during a triceps exercise emphasizes the outer (lateral) head of the triceps brachii. A neutral grip on an EZ bar during a triceps exercise works all three heads of the triceps brachii.
summary: This chunk provides guidance on isolating the triceps brachii during exercises by focusing on elbow movement and grip variations.

content: The barbell seated triceps press targets the triceps brachii muscle. The barbell seated triceps press also involves the deltoid and forearm muscles (wrist flexors, wrist extensors). The barbell seated triceps press is not the best choice for people who have elbow or shoulder pain.
summary: This chunk describes the barbell seated triceps press, its target muscles, and potential limitations for individuals with certain injuries.

content: The EZ-Bar Triceps Press offers a variety of grip choices. The Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press targets the triceps brachii. Secondary muscles involved in the Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press include the deltoid and forearm (wrist flexors, wrist extensors). The Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press can be performed with a neutral grip or a pronated grip.
summary: This chunk compares the EZ-Bar and Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press, highlighting grip options and secondary muscle involvement.

content: A neutral grip on the Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press works all three heads of the triceps brachii. A pronated grip on the Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press targets the inner (long) head of the triceps brachii. The Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press requires a narrow grip on the dumbbell. The elbows should flare outward during the Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press. The vertical position of the arm during the Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press stretches the inner long head of the triceps brachii. In order to isolate the triceps brachii during the Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press, motion should occur only at the elbow joints. The upper arms should remain vertical during the Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press.
summary: This chunk provides detailed instructions for performing the Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press, emphasizing proper form and muscle isolation.

content: Any overhead triceps-extension exercise places the shoulder joint in a position that makes it vulnerable to injury. The One-Arm Seated Triceps Press emphasizes the outer (lateral) head of the triceps brachii. The One-Arm Seated Triceps Press is performed with one arm at a time while holding a dumbbell with the palm facing forward.
summary: This chunk describes the One-Arm Seated Triceps Press, highlighting its focus on the lateral head and potential shoulder joint risks.

content: The Close-Grip Bench Press targets the triceps brachii and pectoralis major. Secondary muscles involved in the Close-Grip Bench Press include the anterior deltoid. The Close-Grip Bench Press is performed with a 6-inch (15 cm) overhand grip on the bar. To target the triceps brachii, hand spacing should be narrower than shoulder width. An underhand (supinated) grip on the bar also targets the triceps brachii, but this grip requires the hands to be spaced wide apart.
summary: This chunk details the Close-Grip Bench Press, its target muscles, and grip recommendations for maximizing triceps activation.

content: The Close-Grip Bench Press targets the triceps brachii and pectoralis major. Secondary muscles involved in the Close-Grip Bench Press include the anterior deltoid. The Close-Grip Bench Press is performed with a 6-inch (15 cm) overhand grip on the bar. To target the triceps brachii, hand spacing should be narrower than shoulder width. Keep your elbows close to your sides to emphasize the triceps brachii, not the chest. A full range of motion (achieving full lockout) is required for maximizing triceps brachii effort.
summary: The Close-Grip Bench Press primarily targets the triceps brachii and pectoralis major, with a narrower grip than a standard bench press to emphasize the triceps.

content: Performing this exercise using an underhand (palms facing up) grip with your hands spaced more than shoulder-width apart also targets the triceps brachii. A neutral (thumb forward) grip works all sections of the triceps brachii. Rotating the dumbbell so your palm faces up targets the outer (lateral) head.
summary: Alternative grips for targeting the triceps brachii include underhand, neutral, and palm-up variations.

content: Holding a dumbbell in one hand, bend forward at the waist and support your torso by resting your free hand on a bench or on your knee. Begin with your upper arm parallel to the floor and your elbow bent 90 degrees. Raise the dumbbell upward, straightening your arm until your elbow locks out.
summary: Instructions for performing a single-arm dumbbell extension, a triceps isolation exercise.

content: The primary muscle involved is the triceps brachii. Secondary muscles involved are the posterior deltoid and latissimus dorsi.
summary: The primary muscle targeted in a single-arm dumbbell extension is the triceps brachii, with secondary involvement from the posterior deltoid and latissimus dorsi.

content: Keep your upper arm parallel to the floor and your elbow close to your side. To isolate the triceps brachii, the shoulder should remain stiff. Movement should occur at the elbow. Because of the effect of gravity, resistance is variable and increases as the dumbbell is raised upward. Your torso should be slightly above parallel to the floor. If you stand too upright, you can’t perform the exercise effectively.
summary: Form tips for a single-arm dumbbell extension, emphasizing proper elbow and shoulder positioning and torso angle.

content: Perform this exercise while using a D-handle attached to a low pulley. Unlike the dumbbell kickback, in which the resistance varies during the lift, the cable provides uniform resistance throughout the movement.
summary: Instructions for performing a cable triceps extension, highlighting the consistent resistance provided by the cable.

content: The wrist curl exercise targets the forearms. To perform a wrist curl, sit on the edge of a bench, grasp a barbell with a shoulder-width underhand grip, and rest the backs of your forearms on your thighs. Lower the bar by bending your wrists toward the floor during a wrist curl. Curl the weight up by using wrist flexion during a wrist curl.
summary: Instructions for performing a wrist curl, emphasizing the underhand grip and movement pattern.

content: The primary muscles involved in the wrist curl are the palmaris longus, flexor carpi radialis, and flexor carpi ulnaris. Secondary muscles involved in the wrist curl are the flexor digitorum superficialis, flexor digitorum profundus, and flexor pollicis longus.
summary: The primary and secondary muscles involved in the wrist curl exercise.

content: The ideal hand spacing for a wrist curl is shoulder width or slightly narrower. Your hands should be directly in line with your forearms during a wrist curl to minimize unnecessary stress in your wrist joints. A supinated (palms facing up) grip is required for the wrist curl exercise. You may place your thumbs either under or over the bar during a wrist curl, depending on personal preference. A thumbless grip allows you to lower the bar farther during a wrist curl, increasing the range of motion.
summary: Form tips for a wrist curl, including hand spacing, grip, and thumb position.

content: Letting the bar roll down your fingers during the lowering phase of the repetition increases the range of motion during a wrist curl. As the bar is curled up, the finger flexors work as you finger-curl the bar into your palm, and then the forearm flexors work as you curl your wrist up during a wrist curl. Because the finger flexors make up a significant portion of the forearm muscles, this extended repetition is more effective for building forearm mass during a wrist curl.
summary: Techniques for maximizing range of motion and muscle activation during a wrist curl.

content: Changing the position of your forearms in relation to the floor alters the resistance and adjusts the focus of the exercise during a wrist curl. When your forearms are flat and parallel to the floor during a wrist curl, resistance is maximal at the beginning and decreases as the bar is lifted up. When your forearms are angled to the floor so that your elbows are higher than your wrists during a wrist curl, resistance is minimal at the start and increases as the bar is curled up. This trajectory is more effective at maximizing forearm contraction during a wrist curl.
summary: How altering forearm angle during a wrist curl affects resistance and muscle activation.

content: You may support your forearms in several ways during a wrist curl: between your legs on a flat bench, on top of your thighs while seated on a bench, or on the incline pad of a preacher bench.
summary: Different ways to support your forearms during a wrist curl.

content: Changing the position of your forearms in relation to the floor alters the resistance and adjusts the focus of the exercise during a wrist curl. content: You may support your forearms in several ways during a wrist curl: between your legs on a flat bench, on top of your thighs while seated on a bench, or on the incline pad of a preacher bench.

content: The dumbbell wrist curl is performed with one arm at a time while holding a dumbbell. The preacher bench wrist curl is performed with your forearms resting on the incline pad of a preacher bench.
summary: Dumbbell and preacher bench variations exist for wrist curls.

content: To perform a barbell standing rear wrist curl, stand upright, grasp a barbell at arms’ length behind your hips using a shoulder-width overhand grip. Curl the bar by bending your wrists upward and backward during a barbell standing rear wrist curl. Lower the weight toward the floor using wrist extension during a barbell standing rear wrist curl.
summary: The barbell standing rear wrist curl involves a standing position and upward wrist curl motion.

content: The primary muscles involved in the reverse wrist curl are the palmaris longus, flexor carpi radialis, and flexor carpi ulnaris. Secondary muscles involved in the reverse wrist curl include the flexor digitorum superficialis, flexor digitorum profundus, and flexor pollicis longus.
summary: The reverse wrist curl primarily targets the palmaris longus, flexor carpi radialis, and flexor carpi ulnaris, with secondary involvement from other forearm flexors.

content: The ideal hand spacing for the reverse wrist curl is shoulder width or slightly wider. Hands should be directly in line with the forearms during the reverse wrist curl to minimize stress on the wrist joints. The reverse wrist curl requires a pronated (overhand) grip. The thumbs can be placed either under or over the bar during the reverse wrist curl.
summary: Proper hand placement and grip are crucial for the reverse wrist curl, with a pronated grip and thumb positioning options.

content: Letting the bar roll down the fingers during the lowering phase of the repetition increases the range of motion in the reverse wrist curl. A thumbless grip allows for the bar to roll down the fingers during the lowering phase of the repetition.
summary: Allowing the bar to roll down the fingers during the reverse wrist curl enhances range of motion.

content: As the bar is curled upward, the finger flexors work as you finger-curl the bar into your palm, and then the forearm flexors work as you curl your wrist up. Because the finger flexors make up a significant portion of the forearm muscles, this extended repetition is more effective for building forearm mass.
summary: The reverse wrist curl engages both finger and forearm flexors, maximizing muscle activation and mass building.

content: The trajectory of the reverse wrist curl is vertical to the floor, with resistance being minimal at the start and increasing as the bar is curled up. The elbows are held straight during the reverse wrist curl. Slightly bending your knees eases the passage of the bar behind the thighs during the reverse wrist curl.
summary: The reverse wrist curl follows a vertical trajectory with increasing resistance, requiring straight elbows and potential knee bend for comfort.

content: The primary muscles involved in the reverse wrist curl are the extensor carpi radialis longus, extensor carpi radialis brevis, and extensor carpi ulnaris. Secondary muscles involved in the reverse wrist curl include the extensor digitorum, extensor pollicis longus, extensor pollicis brevis, extensor indicis, flexor digitorum superficialis, flexor digitorum profundus, and flexor pollicis longus.
summary: The reverse wrist curl primarily targets the extensor carpi muscles, with secondary involvement from both extensor and flexor muscles.

content: The ideal hand spacing for the reverse wrist curl is shoulder width or narrower. Hands should be directly in line with the forearms during the reverse wrist curl. The reverse wrist curl requires a pronated (palms facing down) grip with thumbs around the bar.
summary: Hand spacing and grip are important for the reverse wrist curl, with a pronated grip and thumbs around the bar.

content: To maximize forearm involvement during the barbell reverse curl, achieve a full range of motion at the wrists.
summary: Full wrist range of motion is essential for maximizing forearm engagement during the barbell reverse curl.

content: The barbell reverse curl targets the extensor carpi radialis longus, extensor carpi radialis brevis, extensor carpi ulnaris, extensor digitorum, extensor pollicis longus, extensor pollicis brevis, and extensor indicis. Secondary muscles involved in the barbell reverse curl include the biceps brachii, brachioradialis, and brachialis.
summary: The barbell reverse curl primarily targets the extensor carpi muscles, with secondary involvement from the biceps brachii, brachioradialis, and brachialis.

content: The barbell reverse curl targets the extensor carpi radialis longus, extensor carpi radialis brevis, extensor carpi ulnaris, extensor digitorum, extensor pollicis longus, extensor pollicis brevis, and extensor indicis. Secondary muscles involved in the barbell reverse curl include the biceps brachii, brachioradialis, and brachialis. The ideal hand spacing for the barbell reverse curl is shoulder width with hands directly in line with the forearms. The barbell reverse curl requires a pronated (palms facing down) grip with thumbs around the bar. To maximize forearm involvement during the barbell reverse curl, achieve a full range of motion at the wrists. The barbell reverse curl involves cocking the wrists back into full extension as the bar is raised and flexing the wrists down as the weight is lowered. Resistance in the barbell reverse curl increases as the bar is raised due to gravity. To ensure maximum forearm effort during the barbell reverse curl, delay the wrist-extension curl until the forearms are parallel to the floor.
summary: The barbell reverse curl primarily targets the forearm extensors, with secondary involvement from the biceps and brachioradialis. Proper form emphasizes wrist movement and full range of motion.

content: The dumbbell reverse curl is a variation of the barbell reverse curl performed with one arm at a time while holding a dumbbell in each hand with a pronated (overhand) grip. The wrist roller is a variation of the reverse curl that involves attaching a weight plate to a broomstick and raising the weight by wrist-curling the rope around the stick, alternating hand to hand.
summary: Variations of the reverse curl include the dumbbell reverse curl and the wrist roller, both targeting forearm muscles.

content: The hammer curl involves holding a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing inward and thumbs pointing forward.
summary: The hammer curl is a variation targeting the brachialis and brachioradialis muscles.

content: The leg is divided into the upper leg (thigh) and lower leg (calf). The upper leg consists of one bone, the femur, whereas the lower leg consists of two bones, the tibia and the fibula. The knee is a hinge joint formed at the junction between the femur and the tibia. Two movements occur at the knee joint: flexion and extension. During knee flexion, the lower leg bends toward the back of the thigh. During knee extension, the lower leg moves away from the thigh so the leg becomes straight.
summary: The leg is composed of the thigh and calf, with the knee being a hinge joint allowing for flexion and extension.

content: The hip is a ball-and-socket joint between the upper end of the femur and the pelvic bone. Six main movements occur at the hip joint: flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, internal rotation, and external rotation. During hip flexion the thigh bends up toward the abdomen, whereas during hip extension the thigh moves backward toward the buttocks. The thighs separate during hip abduction and come together during hip adduction.
summary: The hip is a ball-and-socket joint enabling a wide range of motion, including flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, and rotation.

content: The ankle is a hinge-type joint between the lower tibia and fibula and the talus bone in the foot. During ankle dorsiflexion, the toes lift off the floor and the foot moves toward the shin. During ankle plantar flexion, the heel lifts off the floor and the foot moves away from the shin.
summary: The ankle is a hinge joint allowing for dorsiflexion (toes up) and plantar flexion (toes down).

content: The quadriceps femoris, located in front of the thigh, has four separate heads: the rectus femoris, the vastus medialis, the vastus lateralis, and the vastus intermedius. The four heads of the quadriceps merge together, attach onto the patella (knee cap), and then insert via a single patellar tendon onto the tibia, just below the knee joint. The main function of the quadriceps is to extend the knee and straighten the leg. Because the rectus femoris arises from the pelvic bone, contraction of this muscle also flexes the hip joint.
summary: The quadriceps muscle group, consisting of four heads, is responsible for knee extension and hip flexion.

content: The hamstrings, located behind the thigh, is a group of three muscles that originate from the ischium bone of the pelvis. The biceps femoris passes behind the outer aspect of the thigh to attach to the head of the fibula bone, just below the knee. The semimembranosus passes behind the inner aspect of the thigh, attaching to the upper tibia bone behind the knee. The semitendinosus passes behind the inner aspect of the thigh, attaching to the upper tibia bone adjacent to the semimembranosus. All three hamstring muscles span both the knee and hip joints. Therefore, they serve dual functions: flexion of the knee and extension of the hip.
summary: The hamstrings, comprising three muscles, are responsible for knee flexion and hip extension.

content: The gluteus maximus arises from a large area on the rear of the pelvic bone, passes down behind the hip joint, and attaches to the upper femur. This powerful muscle causes hip extension. Exercises that are good for building the gluteal muscles are the squat, deadlift, and lunge.
summary: The gluteus maximus is the primary hip extensor muscle, strengthened through exercises like squats, deadlifts, and lunges.

content: Other muscles that move the hip joint include the following: hip adductors (inner thigh): gracilis, adductor longus, adductor magnus, and adductor brevis, hip abductors: tensor fasciae latae, gluteus medius, and gluteus minimus, hip flexors: sartorius, iliopsoas, and rectus femoris, and hip extensor: gluteus maximus.
summary: Various muscles contribute to hip movement, including adductors, abductors, flexors, and extensors.

content: The lower leg contains 10 muscles. The calf comprises two muscles. The gastrocnemius is the visible muscle of the calf.
summary: The lower leg has 10 muscles, with the calf consisting of two primary muscles, including the visible gastrocnemius.

content: Exercises that are good for building the gluteal muscles are the squat, deadlift, and lunge.
summary: Exercises targeting gluteal muscle growth include squats, deadlifts, and lunges.

content: Other muscles that move the hip joint include the following: hip adductors (inner thigh): gracilis, adductor longus, adductor magnus, and adductor brevis, hip abductors: tensor fasciae latae, gluteus medius, and gluteus minimus, hip flexors: sartorius, iliopsoas, and rectus femoris, and hip extensor: gluteus maximus.
summary: Various muscles contribute to hip movement, including adductors, abductors, flexors, and extensors.

content: The lower leg contains 10 muscles.
summary: The lower leg comprises ten muscles.

content: The calf comprises two muscles. The gastrocnemius is the visible muscle of the calf. The two heads of the gastrocnemius—medial and lateral—arise from the rear of the femur bone, immediately above the knee joint. The soleus arises from the rear aspect of the tibia and lies underneath the gastrocnemius. The tendons of the gastrocnemius and soleus fuse to form the Achilles tendon, which passes behind the ankle joint and attaches to the calcaneus (heel bone).
summary: The calf consists of the gastrocnemius and soleus muscles, which contribute to plantar flexion and form the Achilles tendon.

content: The calf muscles cause plantar flexion of the ankle, the movement required for standing on tiptoes. The relative contribution of the two calf muscles depends on the angle of knee flexion. The gastrocnemius is the prime mover when the leg is straight, and the soleus becomes more active as the knee bends.
summary: Calf muscles facilitate plantar flexion, with gastrocnemius dominant in straight legs and soleus more active with bent knees.

content: The gastrocnemius crosses both the knee and ankle joints and therefore serves a double function: knee flexion and ankle flexion.
summary: The gastrocnemius muscle crosses both the knee and ankle joints, contributing to both knee flexion and ankle flexion.

content: The tibialis anterior muscle is responsible for ankle extension (dorsiflexion). The peroneus longus and peroneus brevis muscles are responsible for ankle eversion. The tibialis posterior muscle is responsible for ankle inversion.
summary: Various muscles control ankle movement, including tibialis anterior for dorsiflexion, peroneus muscles for eversion, and tibialis posterior for inversion.

content: The flexor digitorum longus, flexor hallucis longus, extensor digitorum longus, and extensor hallucis longus muscles are responsible for toe flexion and extension.
summary: Specific muscles control toe movement, including flexors and extensors for flexion and extension.

content: The quadriceps consist of the rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, and vastus intermedius muscles.
summary: The quadriceps muscle group comprises four muscles: rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, and vastus intermedius.

content: Pointing your toes directly upward during a leg extension hits all sections of the quadriceps equally. Pointing your toes inward during a leg extension internally rotates the tibia to target the inner quadriceps teardrop, the vastus medialis. Pointing your toes outward during a leg extension externally rotates the tibia to hit the outer quadriceps, the vastus lateralis.
summary: Toe direction during leg extensions influences quadriceps muscle activation, targeting different sections based on inward, outward, or neutral toe pointing.

content: Placing your feet close together during a leg extension tends to target the outer quadriceps, and a wider spacing focuses effort a little more on the inner quadriceps.
summary: Foot placement during leg extensions affects muscle activation, with closer spacing favoring outer quadriceps and wider spacing emphasizing inner quadriceps.

content: Adjusting the backrest so that the backs of your knees fit snugly against the front edge of the seat and your whole thighs are supported during a leg extension ensures proper form.
summary: Proper leg extension form involves adjusting the backrest for knee and thigh support.

content: Leaning your torso backward or raising your buttocks off the seat during a leg extension extends the hip joint, stretching the rectus femoris and making this section of the quadriceps work harder.
summary: Leaning back during leg extensions engages the rectus femoris more by extending the hip joint.

content: The arc of motion during a leg extension should be approximately 90 degrees. Forcefully contract the quadriceps at the top of the movement when the knees are fully straight during a leg extension. Do not bend the knees beyond 90 degrees during a leg extension to avoid excess stress on the patella (kneecap).
summary: Leg extension technique involves a 90-degree range of motion, full quadriceps contraction at the top, and avoiding knee bending beyond 90 degrees to protect the kneecap.

content: Resistance is fairly uniform during a leg extension, but on many new machines the resistance increases slightly as the weight is lifted.
summary: Leg extension resistance is generally uniform, but some machines may have slight increases as weight is lifted.

content: Less resistance at the starting position during a leg extension minimizes stress across the kneecaps with the knees bent.
summary: Reduced starting resistance in leg extensions minimizes stress on the kneecaps during the bent knee phase.

content: Performing a leg extension one leg at a time improves focus. The unilateral leg extension is particularly useful for improving thigh asymmetry or aiding in rehabilitation when one leg is injured.
summary: Unilateral leg extensions enhance focus and are beneficial for addressing thigh asymmetry or aiding rehabilitation.

content: The squat is a strength exercise that utilizes nearly every muscle in the body, but for bodybuilding purposes the focus is on the thigh muscles.
summary: Squats engage numerous muscles, but bodybuilding applications emphasize thigh muscle development.

content: The primary muscles involved in the squat are the quadriceps (rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, vastus intermedius), and gluteus maximus.
summary: The primary squat muscles are the quadriceps and gluteus maximus.

content: Secondary muscles involved in the squat include the hamstrings (semitendinosus, semimembranosus, biceps femoris), adductors (longus, magnus, brevis), gracilis, tensor fasciae latae, spinal erectors (sacrospinalis), and abdominals (rectus abdominis, external oblique, internal oblique).
summary: Secondary muscles activated during squats include hamstrings, adductors, gracilis, tensor fasciae latae, spinal erectors, and abdominals.

content: A narrow stance during a squat shifts focus to the outer quadriceps, the vastus lateralis, and abductors (tensor fasciae latae). A shoulder-width stance during a squat targets the whole thigh. A wider stance during a squat places more emphasis on the inner quadriceps, adductor muscles, and sartorius.
summary: Squat stance width influences muscle activation, with narrow stances favoring outer quads and abductors, shoulder-width stances targeting the whole thigh, and wider stances emphasizing inner quads and adductors.

content: Point your toes forward or slightly outward in the same direction as your thighs and knees during a squat. Placing a 1-inch (2.5 cm) block under both heels during a squat shifts the weight forward, placing more emphasis on the quadriceps and less on the gluteals. Positioning the bar lower on the trapezius and shoulders during a squat improves balance while shifting focus to the gluteals.
summary: Proper squat technique involves toe direction, heel elevation for quadriceps emphasis, and bar positioning for balance and gluteal focus.

content: Keep your spine straight and head up at all times during a squat.
summary: Maintaining a straight spine and head position is crucial during squats.

content: A wider stance during a squat places more emphasis on the inner quadriceps, adductor muscles, and sartorius. A narrow stance during a squat shifts focus to the outer quadriceps (vastus lateralis) and abductors (tensor fasciae latae). Placing your feet close to your body during a squat emphasizes the quadriceps. Positioning your feet forward away from your body during a squat switches the focus to the gluteals and hamstrings. A shoulder-width stance during a squat targets the whole thigh. A wider foot spacing during the leg press places more emphasis on the inner quadriceps (vastus medialis), adductor muscles, and sartorius. Placing your feet close together during the leg press shifts focus to the outer quadriceps (vastus lateralis) and abductors (tensor fasciae latae).
summary: This chunk describes how stance width affects muscle emphasis in both squats and leg presses.

content: Point your toes forward or slightly outward in the same direction as your thighs and knees during a squat. Point your toes forward or slightly outward in the same direction as your thighs and knees during a squat.
summary: This chunk provides guidance on proper toe positioning during squats.

content: Placing a 1-inch (2.5 cm) block under both heels during a squat shifts the weight forward, placing more emphasis on the quadriceps and less on the gluteals. Positioning the bar lower on the trapezius and shoulders during a squat improves balance while shifting focus to the gluteals.
summary: This chunk explains how heel elevation and bar position affect squat muscle emphasis.

content: Keep your spine straight and head up at all times during a squat. Do not bend your torso forward during a squat, as this can cause back injury.
summary: This chunk emphasizes proper spinal alignment during squats to prevent injury.

content: As you lower the weight during a squat, stop when your knees bend to a 90-degree angle and your thighs are parallel to the floor. Squatting below parallel increases the risk of injury to the knees and spine. At the top of the movement during a squat, stopping a few degrees short of full lockout keeps tension on the quadriceps.
summary: This chunk outlines proper squat depth and technique to minimize injury risk.

content: Inhale deeply during the downward phase of a squat and exhale on the way up. Place your hands equidistant from the center of the bar and maintain a firm overhand grip during a squat to ensure that the bar stays in the unlocked position.
summary: This chunk provides breathing and grip instructions for squats.

content: The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. The quadriceps muscles involved in the squat are the rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, and vastus intermedius.
