Secondary muscles involved in the squat are the gluteus maximus, hamstrings (semitendinosus, semimembranosus, biceps femoris), adductors (longus, magnus, brevis), tensor fasciae latae, spinal erectors (sacrospinalis), and abdominals (rectus abdominis, external oblique, internal oblique).
summary: This chunk describes the primary and secondary muscles targeted during a squat.

content: Performing the squat with the barbell held across the front of your shoulders shifts the emphasis away from the gluteals and toward the quadriceps. The front squat poses a higher degree of difficulty and requires lighter weights.
summary: This chunk explains the differences between a conventional and front squat.

content: The machine squat provides better balance and improves safety compared to the barbell squat. The Smith machine provides a single plane of vertical motion that can help focus your effort during the exercise.
summary: This chunk compares different squat variations and their benefits.

content: The primary muscles involved in the leg press are the quadriceps (rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, vastus intermedius). Secondary muscles involved in the leg press are the gluteus maximus, hamstrings (semitendinosus, semimembranosus, biceps femoris), adductors (longus, magnus, brevis), gracilis, and tensor fasciae latae.
summary: This chunk describes the primary and secondary muscles targeted during a leg press.

content: Placing your feet low on the footplate during the leg press emphasizes the quadriceps. Positioning your feet high on the footplate during the leg press switches the focus to the gluteals and hamstrings. Placing your feet shoulder-width apart during the leg press targets the whole thigh.
summary: This chunk explains how foot placement affects muscle emphasis during a leg press.

content: Pushing the weight up using the balls of your feet and allowing your heels to rise off the footplate as the weight is lowered during the leg press targets the quadriceps and reduces load across your kneecaps. Pushing the weight through the heels of your feet during the leg press targets the hamstrings and gluteals.
summary: This chunk describes how foot engagement during a leg press affects muscle activation.

content: The angle your torso makes with your legs influences muscular focus and the amount of stress through your lower back during the leg press. Emphasis is placed on the gluteals and hamstrings when the angle between the seat and backrest is 90 degrees during the leg press, but this acute angle places more stress on your lower back.
summary: This chunk explains how torso angle affects muscle emphasis and lower back stress during a leg press.

content: Placing your feet close together during the leg press shifts focus to the outer quadriceps (vastus lateralis) and abductors (tensor fasciae latae). Placing your feet close together during a hack squat shifts focus to the outer quadriceps (vastus lateralis) and abductors (tensor fasciae latae).
summary: Foot placement in both leg press and hack squat variations influences muscle emphasis, favoring outer quadriceps and abductors with close feet.

content: Pushing the weight up using the balls of your feet and allowing your heels to rise off the footplate as the weight is lowered during the leg press targets the quadriceps and reduces load across your kneecaps. Pushing the weight using the forefoot and allowing your heels to rise off the footplate as the weight is lowered helps isolate the quadriceps and reduce stress across the kneecaps.
summary: Foot technique during leg press, emphasizing forefoot push and heel lift, isolates quadriceps and reduces knee stress.

content: Pushing the weight through the heels of your feet during the leg press targets the hamstrings and gluteals. Performing a hack squat while facing the machine switches the focus to the glutes and hamstrings.
summary: Heel-driven leg press and reverse hack squat emphasize hamstring and glute activation.

content: The angle your torso makes with your legs influences muscular focus and the amount of stress through your lower back during the leg press. Emphasis is placed on the gluteals and hamstrings when the angle between the seat and backrest is 90 degrees during the leg press, but this acute angle places more stress on your lower back. If the backrest is tilted lower toward the floor during the leg press, your torso leans back; this places less stress across your lower spine and more emphasis on the quadriceps.
summary: Leg press torso angle affects muscle emphasis and lower back stress. 90-degree angle targets glutes and hamstrings, while a lower backrest emphasizes quads and reduces back stress.

content: Stopping a few degrees short of full lockout at the top of the movement during the leg press keeps tension on the quadriceps. Stopping a few degrees short of full lockout at the top of the movement during a hack squat keeps tension on the quadriceps.
summary: Partial range of motion at the top of leg press and hack squat movements maintains quadriceps tension.

content: Compared to the barbell squat, the leg press reduces the axial load on your spine and reduces the risk of backache. Compared to the barbell squat, the hack squat provides support for your spine.
summary: Both leg press and hack squat offer reduced spinal load compared to barbell squats, minimizing back strain.

content: The leg press emphasizes the quadriceps rather than the gluteals. The hack squat places more emphasis on the quadriceps and less on the gluteals.
summary: Both leg press and hack squat primarily target quadriceps, with less emphasis on gluteals compared to other exercises.

content: Perform the one-leg press with one leg at a time to focus effort on a lagging thigh or to protect an injured leg.
summary: One-leg press isolates each leg, useful for addressing imbalances or protecting injuries.

content: The hack squat targets the quadriceps, gluteus maximus, hamstrings, adductors, gracilis, and tensor fasciae latae.
summary: Hack squat engages a wide range of muscles in the thighs and hips.

content: To perform a hack squat, place your back against the backrest and shoulders under the pads of a hack squat machine. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart on the footplate with your toes pointing forward. Slowly lower the weight while bending your knees to 90 degrees. Push the weight back to the starting position by straightening your legs.
summary: Instructions for performing a hack squat, including setup, movement, and range of motion.

content: Placing your feet shoulder-width apart during a hack squat targets the whole thigh. A wider foot spacing during a hack squat places more emphasis on the inner quadriceps, adductor muscles, and sartorius. Point your toes forward or slightly outward in the same direction as your thighs and knees during a hack squat.
summary: Foot placement and toe direction in the hack squat influence muscle emphasis, targeting different thigh regions.

content: Placing your feet low on the footplate during a hack squat emphasizes the quadriceps. Placing your feet higher on the footplate during a hack squat requires more effort from the gluteals and hamstrings.
summary: Footplate height in the hack squat affects muscle activation, with lower placement emphasizing quads and higher placement engaging glutes and hamstrings.

content: Keep your spine flat against the backrest during a hack squat.
summary: Maintaining a flat back during the hack squat ensures proper form and minimizes spinal stress.

content: To perform a dumbbell squat, squat while holding a dumbbell in each hand at arms’ length at your sides. The strength of your grip will limit the amount of weight you can use in a dumbbell squat.
summary: Instructions for performing a dumbbell squat, noting the grip strength limitation on weight selection.

content: To perform a lunge, stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and hold a dumbbell in each hand at arms’ length by your sides. Step forward with one foot and bend your knee until your leading thigh is parallel to the floor during a lunge. Return to the starting position and repeat with the opposite leg during a lunge.
summary: Instructions for performing a lunge, including stance, movement, and repetition.

content: The quadriceps (rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, vastus intermedius) is a primary muscle involved in the lunge. The gluteus maximus is a primary muscle involved in the lunge. The hamstrings (semitendinosus, semimembranosus, biceps femoris) are a secondary muscle involved in the lunge. The adductors (longus, magnus, brevis) are a secondary muscle involved in the lunge. The gracilis is a secondary muscle involved in the lunge. The tensor fasciae latae is a secondary muscle involved in the lunge.
summary: Muscle groups involved in the lunge, highlighting primary and secondary muscle activation.

content: A stable, shoulder-width stance best maintains balance during a lunge.
summary: Proper stance width is crucial for balance and stability during lunges.

content: The hamstrings (semitendinosus, semimembranosus, biceps femoris) are a secondary muscle involved in the lunge. The adductors (longus, magnus, brevis) are a secondary muscle involved in the lunge. The gracilis is a secondary muscle involved in the lunge. The tensor fasciae latae is a secondary muscle involved in the lunge. A stable, shoulder-width stance best maintains balance during a lunge. Point your toes straight ahead or slightly outward as you step forward during a lunge. Keep your back foot fixed to the floor in the same spot during a lunge. A shorter step during a lunge targets the quadriceps. A larger step during a lunge places the emphasis on the gluteals and hamstrings.
summary: The lunge engages various muscles, with step length influencing target muscle groups. Proper form emphasizes balance and stability.

content: As you lunge forward, place your body weight on the leading leg. Keep your torso upright and your back straight during a lunge. During the lunge, your knee should bend 90 degrees and your thigh should be parallel to the floor. The lunge requires a lighter weight than most other leg exercises. Using a weight that is too heavy during a lunge may cause pain in the kneecaps.
summary: Lunge technique emphasizes proper posture and weight distribution, with lighter weights recommended to avoid knee strain.

content: Instead of holding a dumbbell in each hand during a lunge, rest a barbell across your shoulders. Maintaining balance is more difficult during a barbell lunge compared with a dumbbell lunge. Instead of returning to the starting position after a lunge, perform one lunge after another, alternating legs so that you walk the length of the floor. Perform the lunge using a Smith machine, which provides stability and balance.
summary: Lunge variations include barbell and Smith machine options, each offering different levels of stability and challenge.

content: To perform a lying leg curl, lie facedown on the machine and hook your heels under the roller pads. Curl the weight by bending your knees and raise your heels toward your buttocks during a lying leg curl. Lower the weight back down to the starting position during a lying leg curl.
summary: The lying leg curl targets the hamstrings through a controlled bending and extending motion.

content: The semitendinosus, semimembranosus, and biceps femoris are primary muscles involved in the hamstring variation. The gluteus maximus and gastrocnemius are secondary muscles involved in the hamstring variation. Pointing your toes straight during a hamstring variation targets all three hamstring muscles. Pointing your toes inward during a hamstring variation emphasizes the inner hamstrings (the semimembranosus and semitendinosus). Pointing your toes outward during a hamstring variation focuses effort on the outer hamstrings (the biceps femoris).
summary: The hamstring variation targets the primary hamstring muscles, with toe direction influencing muscle emphasis.

content: Keeping your ankles bent 90 degrees (dorsiflexion) during a hamstring variation minimizes contribution from the calf muscles and thereby helps isolate the hamstrings. Pointing your feet (plantar flexion) during a hamstring variation allows the calf muscles to participate in the exercise. Placing your feet hip-width apart is the standard position for a hamstring variation. Wide foot spacing during a hamstring variation targets the inner hamstrings (the semimembranosus and semitendinosus). Narrow foot spacing during a hamstring variation emphasizes effort of the outer hamstrings (the biceps femoris).
summary: Foot position and ankle angle in the hamstring variation influence muscle activation and isolation.

content: The padded surface of most machines is angled at hip level, bending your torso forward slightly during a hamstring variation. This body position tilts your pelvis and stretches the hamstrings, thereby helping to isolate the muscles during a hamstring variation. Keep your spine straight and do not raise your chest during a hamstring variation. Bend your knees as far as possible during the upward phase of a hamstring variation. Stop a few degrees short of full extension at the bottom of the movement during a hamstring variation to keep tension on the hamstrings and minimize stress across the knee joints.
summary: Proper form during the hamstring variation involves torso positioning, spinal alignment, and controlled movement to maximize muscle engagement and minimize joint stress.

content: Resistance is fairly uniform during a hamstring variation, but on many new machines the resistance is lower at the starting position, where the hamstrings are fully stretched and most vulnerable to injury.
summary: Resistance characteristics in hamstring variations can vary, with some machines offering lighter resistance at the starting position.

content: The Standing Leg Curl targets the hamstrings, gluteus maximus, and gastrocnemius. The primary hamstrings muscles involved in the Standing Leg Curl are the semitendinosus, semimembranosus, and biceps femoris. Pointing your toes straight down during the Standing Leg Curl targets all three hamstring muscles.
summary: The Standing Leg Curl engages the hamstrings, gluteus maximus, and gastrocnemius, with toe direction influencing hamstring muscle activation.

content: The Standing Leg Curl targets the hamstrings, gluteus maximus, and gastrocnemius. The primary hamstrings muscles involved in the Standing Leg Curl are the semitendinosus, semimembranosus, and biceps femoris. Pointing your toes straight down during the Standing Leg Curl targets all three hamstring muscles. Pointing your toes inward during the Standing Leg Curl emphasizes the inner hamstrings (the semimembranosus and semitendinosus). Pointing your toes outward during the Standing Leg Curl focuses effort on the outer hamstrings (the biceps femoris). Keeping the ankle of the working leg bent 90 degrees during the Standing Leg Curl minimizes the contribution from the calf muscles and helps isolate the hamstrings. The padded surface of most Standing Leg Curl machines is angled at hip level, bending your torso forward slightly. This body position tilts your pelvis and stretches the hamstrings, helping to isolate the muscles. The Standing Leg Curl is performed with one leg at a time, which helps muscle isolation and focus. Resistance is fairly uniform during the Standing Leg Curl, but on many new machines the resistance is lower at the starting position.
summary: The Standing Leg Curl is a hamstring isolation exercise with variations in toe position to target different hamstring heads.

content: The Barbell Stiff-Legged Deadlift targets the gluteus maximus, gluteus medius, vastus lateralis, and hamstrings (biceps femoris, semitendinosus, and semimembranosus). During the Barbell Stiff-Legged Deadlift, stand upright with your feet directly below your hips and hold a barbell at arms’ length. The primary muscles involved in the Stiff-Legged Deadlift are the hamstrings (semitendinosus, semimembranosus, biceps femoris) and gluteus maximus. Secondary muscles involved in the Stiff-Legged Deadlift include the spinal erectors (sacrospinalis) and quadriceps (rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, vastus intermedius). For the Stiff-Legged Deadlift, position your feet directly below your hips. A wider stance during the Stiff-Legged Deadlift places more emphasis on the inner hamstrings. Point your toes directly forward or slightly outward during the Stiff-Legged Deadlift. For the Stiff-Legged Deadlift, grip the dumbbells shoulder-width apart with your arms vertical so that the weights stay clear of your legs. The dumbbells should travel straight up and down and stay close to the body during the Stiff-Legged Deadlift. Your knees may be slightly bent but should be kept stiff during the Stiff-Legged Deadlift to isolate the hamstrings. Keep your back straight throughout the Stiff-Legged Deadlift. Lower the weight until your hamstrings reach a full stretch without rounding your spine during the Stiff-Legged Deadlift. Depending on your flexibility, lower the dumbbells to a point below your knees or just above your ankles during the Stiff-Legged Deadlift. This exercise requires a lighter weight than that used during the standard barbell deadlift for strengthening the lower back.
summary: The Barbell Stiff-Legged Deadlift is a hamstring and glute exercise with variations in stance and toe position to target different muscle groups.

content: The Dumbbell Stiff-Legged Deadlift is performed by standing upright with your feet directly below your hips, holding a dumbbell in each hand at arms’ length. The Kneeling Leg Curl machine supports your nonworking leg and your elbows support your torso. Because your torso is bent forward at the waist during the Kneeling Leg Curl, the hamstrings are stretched.
summary: The Dumbbell Stiff-Legged Deadlift and Kneeling Leg Curl are variations of the stiff-legged deadlift, targeting the hamstrings.

content: The Standing Calf Raise targets the gastrocnemius and soleus muscles. To perform a Standing Calf Raise, stand with your toes on the platform of the machine and your shoulders under the pads. Lower your heels as far as possible for a full stretch during a Standing Calf Raise. Lift the weight by raising your heels as high as possible, keeping your legs straight during a Standing Calf Raise. Slowly lower your heels back down to the starting position during a Standing Calf Raise. Pointing your toes straight ahead during a Standing Calf Raise targets the whole gastrocnemius muscle. Pointing your toes outward during a Standing Calf Raise emphasizes the inner (medial) head of the gastrocnemius muscle. Pointing your toes inward during a Standing Calf Raise targets the outer (lateral) head of the gastrocnemius muscle. Positioning your feet hip-width apart during a Standing Calf Raise targets the whole gastrocnemius muscle. A wide stance during a Standing Calf Raise tends to emphasize the inner (medial) head of the gastrocnemius muscle. A narrow stance during a Standing Calf Raise targets the outer (lateral) head of the gastrocnemius muscle.
summary: The Standing Calf Raise targets the gastrocnemius and soleus muscles, with variations in toe position and stance to emphasize different muscle heads.

content: Stop a few degrees short of full extension at the bottom of the movement during a hamstring variation to keep tension on the hamstrings and minimize stress across the knee joints. Resistance is fairly uniform during a hamstring variation, but on many new machines the resistance is lower at the starting position, where the hamstrings are fully stretched and most vulnerable to injury.
summary: Precautions for hamstring exercises: avoid full extension and be aware of resistance variations.

content: Pointing your toes outward during a Standing Calf Raise emphasizes the inner (medial) head of the gastrocnemius muscle. A wide stance during a Standing Calf Raise tends to emphasize the inner (medial) head of the gastrocnemius muscle. Pointing your toes outward emphasizes the inner (medial) head of the gastrocnemius muscle for the Donkey Calf Raise variation.
summary: Variations emphasizing the inner gastrocnemius head include outward toe pointing and a wide stance.

content: Pointing your toes inward during a Standing Calf Raise targets the outer (lateral) head of the gastrocnemius muscle. A narrow stance during a Standing Calf Raise targets the outer (lateral) head of the gastrocnemius muscle. Pointing your toes inward targets the outer (lateral) head of the gastrocnemius muscle for the Donkey Calf Raise variation.
summary: Variations emphasizing the outer gastrocnemius head include inward toe pointing and a narrow stance.

content: Positioning your feet hip-width apart during a Standing Calf Raise targets the whole gastrocnemius muscle. Positioning your feet hip-width apart targets the whole gastrocnemius muscle for the Donkey Calf Raise variation.
summary: A hip-width stance targets the entire gastrocnemius muscle.

content: Keep your knees stiff and your back straight during a Standing Calf Raise. Keep your spine straight and your torso parallel to the floor for the Donkey Calf Raise variation.
summary: Proper form for calf raises includes a straight back and stiff knees.

content: Keeping your knees locked straight during a Standing Calf Raise stretches the gastrocnemius, which helps to focus effort on the gastrocnemius and minimizes soleus action. Keeping your knees fully straight helps isolate the gastrocnemius for the Donkey Calf Raise variation.
summary: Keeping knees straight isolates the gastrocnemius and minimizes soleus involvement.

content: If your knees bend during a Standing Calf Raise, the soleus contributes to the movement. A slight bend at the knees allows the soleus to contribute to the movement for the Donkey Calf Raise variation.
summary: Knee bending allows the soleus muscle to participate.

content: To maximize the range of motion during a Standing Calf Raise, aim for a full stretch at the bottom of the movement and a full squeeze at the top. To maximize the range of motion, aim for a full stretch at the bottom of the movement and a full squeeze at the top for the Donkey Calf Raise variation.
summary: Full range of motion is achieved with a deep stretch and full contraction.

content: Perform calf raises with one leg at a time during the Dumbbell One-Leg Calf Raise. When you have performed the desired number of repetitions, switch to the other side during the Dumbbell One-Leg Calf Raise.
summary: Dumbbell One-Leg Calf Raises are performed unilaterally, alternating legs.

content: Perform the Smith Machine Calf Raise at the Smith machine while standing on a block of wood that is 3 inches (7.5 cm) thick. Place your toes on a block that is 3 inches (7.5 cm) thick for the Donkey Calf Raise.
summary: Both Smith Machine and Donkey Calf Raises utilize a platform to increase range of motion.

content: Hold a dumbbell in one hand and place the toes of the same-side foot on a block that is 3 inches (7.5 cm) thick for the Dumbbell One-Leg Calf Raise. Lower your heel as far as possible during the Dumbbell One-Leg Calf Raise. Support your torso with your free hand during the Dumbbell One-Leg Calf Raise. Lift the weight by raising your heel as high as you can, keeping your leg straight during the Dumbbell One-Leg Calf Raise. Slowly lower your heel back down to the starting position during the Dumbbell One-Leg Calf Raise.
summary: The Dumbbell One-Leg Calf Raise involves a single leg, dumbbell, and controlled movement.

content: The primary muscle involved in the calves exercise is the Gastrocnemius. The secondary muscle involved in the calves exercise is the Soleus.
summary: The gastrocnemius is the primary calf muscle, with the soleus as a secondary contributor.

content: Pointing your toes straight ahead targets the whole gastrocnemius muscle.
summary: Neutral toe position targets the entire gastrocnemius.

content: The standing block should be thick enough to allow a full range of motion without your heel touching the floor during the downward phase for the variation. To maximize the range of motion, aim for a full stretch at the bottom of the movement and a full squeeze at the top for the variation. Keep your spine straight and your torso parallel to the floor for the variation. Keeping your knees fully straight helps isolate the gastrocnemius for the variation. A slight bend at the knees allows the soleus to contribute to the movement for the variation.
summary: Instructions and considerations for performing a standing calf raise variation.

content: Perform the Machine Donkey Calf Raise while using a machine that transmits the weight through a pad resting across your lower back. To perform a Machine Calf Raise, sit in a leg press machine, place the balls of your feet on the edge of the footplate, lower the weight as far as possible keeping your knees straight, push the weight up as far as you can contracting your calf muscles, and slowly lower the weight back down to the starting position. The Machine Calf Raise targets the gastrocnemius and soleus muscles. Resistance in the Machine Calf Raise is transmitted through the footplate.
summary: Description of the Machine Donkey Calf Raise and Machine Calf Raise exercises.

content: Pointing your toes straight ahead during a Machine Calf Raise targets the whole gastrocnemius muscle. Pointing your toes outward during a Machine Calf Raise emphasizes the inner (medial) head of the gastrocnemius. Pointing your toes inward during a Machine Calf Raise targets the outer (lateral) head of the gastrocnemius. Positioning your feet hip-width apart during a Machine Calf Raise targets the whole gastrocnemius muscle. A wide stance during a Machine Calf Raise emphasizes the inner (medial) head. A narrow stance during a Machine Calf Raise targets the outer (lateral) head.
summary: How toe position affects muscle activation during a Machine Calf Raise.

content: Keeping your knees stiff during a Machine Calf Raise ensures movement occurs exclusively at the ankle. Keeping your knees fully straight during a Machine Calf Raise helps isolate the gastrocnemius. A slight bend at the knees during a Machine Calf Raise allows the soleus to contribute to the movement. To maximize the range of motion during a Machine Calf Raise, aim for a full stretch at the bottom of the movement and a full squeeze at the top.
summary: Knee position and range of motion considerations for a Machine Calf Raise.

content: To perform a calf-sled machine exercise, lie supine on the sled with your shoulders against the pads and your feet on the platform. Place the toes and balls of your feet on the footplate, legs straight, lower your heels for a full stretch, and lift the weight by raising your heels as high as possible. The calf-sled machine typically uses a fixed foot plate with resistance transmitted through a mobile torso sled.
summary: Instructions for performing a calf-sled machine exercise.

content: To perform a Seated Calf Raise, place the balls of your feet on the platform of the seated calf machine, place the pads across your lower thighs, lower your heels as far as possible, lift the weight by raising your heels as high as you can, and slowly lower your heels back down to the starting position. The Seated Calf Raise targets the soleus and gastrocnemius muscles.
summary: Instructions for performing a Seated Calf Raise.

content: Pointing your toes straight ahead targets the whole calf muscle. Pointing your toes outward emphasizes the inner calf. Pointing your toes inward targets the outer section of the calf muscle. Positioning your feet hip-width apart targets the whole calf muscle. A wide stance emphasizes the inner (medial) head of the calf muscle, whereas a narrow stance targets the outer (lateral) head.
summary: How toe position affects muscle activation during calf raises.

content: In the seated position, the bent knee places emphasis on both the soleus and the gastrocnemius. To maximize the range of motion, aim for a full stretch at the bottom of the movement and a full squeeze at the top.
summary: Knee position and range of motion considerations for seated calf raises.

content: The abdominal wall can be divided into two anatomic parts, each of which functions differently. The front wall consists of one muscle, the rectus abdominis (also known as the abs). The rectus abdominis muscle arises from the lower margin of the rib cage and sternum and passes vertically downward to attach on the pubic bone. The two rectus abdominis muscles (one on each side) are encased in a sheath of fascia that forms the central demarcation down the middle of the abs, known as the linea alba. Fascia divisions in the muscles are responsible for the six-pack appearance. The rectus abdominis muscles cause flexion of the trunk, bending the torso forward toward the legs.
summary: Anatomy and function of the rectus abdominis muscle.

content: The front wall consists of one muscle, the rectus abdominis (also known as the abs). The rectus abdominis muscle arises from the lower margin of the rib cage and sternum and passes vertically downward to attach on the pubic bone. The two rectus abdominis muscles (one on each side) are encased in a sheath of fascia that forms the central demarcation down the middle of the abs, known as the linea alba. Fascia divisions in the muscles are responsible for the six-pack appearance. The rectus abdominis muscles cause flexion of the trunk, bending the torso forward toward the legs. The motion is carried out by the upper abs, which pull the rib cage down toward the pelvis, or by the lower abs, which lift the pelvis up toward the chest.
summary: The rectus abdominis muscle, located on the front of the abdomen, is responsible for trunk flexion.

content: The side wall consists of three layers of muscles. The external oblique is the outer visible layer that passes obliquely downward from the rib cage to the pelvic bone. The internal oblique is the middle layer that passes obliquely upward from the pelvic bone to the ribs. The internal oblique lies under the external oblique, and the fibers of the two muscles pass at right angles to one another. The transversus abdominis is the innermost layer that lies horizontally across the abdominal wall. Contraction of the oblique muscles on one side causes the torso to bend sideways. Contraction of the obliques simultaneously on both sides assists the rectus muscle in flexing the trunk and splints the abdominal wall whenever a weight is lifted. Only the outer external oblique is visible.
summary: The abdominal side wall comprises three muscle layers: the external oblique, internal oblique, and transversus abdominis, each contributing to trunk stability and movement.

content: The serratus anterior muscle forms part of the side wall of the chest. The serratus anterior muscle arises from the scapula behind and passes forward around the chest wall to attach to the upper eight ribs. The serrated edge of the serratus anterior muscle emerges from beneath the outer margin of the pectoralis muscle, sending fingerlike projections into the external oblique. The serratus anterior muscle pulls (or protracts) the scapula forward, stabilizing the scapula against the chest wall. The serratus anterior muscle provides an essential accessory function whenever the pectoralis major and latissimus dorsi muscles contract. The serratus anterior muscle can also be targeted during exercises that work the oblique muscles.
summary: The serratus anterior muscle, located on the side of the chest, stabilizes the scapula and assists in shoulder movement.

content: An effective abdominal workout should include exercises that target all areas of your midsection. For your upper abdominals, select crunches or sit-ups. For your lower abdominals, choose from leg raises, knee-ups, or reverse crunches. To complete your workout, target the side wall with twisting maneuvers, oblique crunches, or side bends.
summary: A comprehensive abdominal workout should engage all sections of the core: upper, lower, and obliques.

content: The sit-up exercise involves hooking your feet under the pad of a decline bench and sitting on the bench with your torso upright. In the sit-up exercise, lower your torso backward until it is almost parallel to the floor. Return to the upright position by bending at the waist in the sit-up exercise. The primary muscle involved in the sit-up exercise is the rectus abdominis. Secondary muscles involved in the sit-up exercise include the quadriceps (rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, vastus intermedius) and hip flexors (sartorius, iliopsoas). You may hold your hands together behind your lower back, cross them in front of your chest, or interlock them behind your head during a sit-up. The relative resistance increases as your hands shift from your lower back to your chest to your head during a sit-up. Secure your feet under a roller pad or comparable support during a sit-up. Bend your knees to reduce stress on the lower back during a sit-up. Your torso should be vertically upright in the sitting position, with your abdomen almost touching your thighs during a sit-up. Lower your torso backward until it is almost parallel to the floor, about three-quarters of the way down during a sit-up. Do not lean back too far because stress is placed on the lower back when tension is released from the abdominals during a sit-up. The decline angle of the bench is typically 30 to 45 degrees during a sit-up. Tilting the bench at a steeper angle makes the exercise more difficult during a sit-up. Add resistance by tilting the bench at a steeper angle or holding a weight plate to your chest during a sit-up. Perform the floor sit-up exercise while seated on the floor with your knees bent and your feet secured to the floor.
summary: The sit-up exercise is a classic abdominal exercise targeting the rectus abdominis and other supporting muscles. Proper form and modifications can adjust the difficulty and target specific muscle groups.

content: Do not lean back too far because stress is placed on the lower back when tension is released from the abdominals during a sit-up. The decline angle of the bench is typically 30 to 45 degrees during a sit-up. Tilting the bench at a steeper angle makes the exercise more difficult during a sit-up. Add resistance by tilting the bench at a steeper angle or holding a weight plate to your chest during a sit-up.
summary: Sit-up technique and modifications for increased difficulty.

content: Perform the floor sit-up exercise while seated on the floor with your knees bent and your feet secured to the floor. The crunch exercise targets the upper rectus abdominis. The secondary muscles involved in the crunch are the external oblique and internal oblique. To perform a crunch, lie flat on the floor with hips bent at 90 degrees and hands behind your head. Raise your shoulders off the floor and crunch your chest forward while keeping your lower back in contact with the floor. Lower your shoulders to the starting position.
summary: Description of floor sit-up and crunch exercises.

content: You may position your hands at your sides, across your chest, or interlocked behind your head during a crunch. The resistance of a crunch increases as your hand position shifts from your sides to your chest to your head. You may place your feet on the floor close to your buttocks or elevate them on a bench during a crunch. Elevating your legs increases the resistance of a crunch. Your thighs should be positioned at a 90-degree angle to your torso during a crunch. You may support your lower legs on a flat bench or position your feet on the floor close to your buttocks during a crunch. The crunch motion occurs in the upper spine, and your shoulders rise a few inches off the floor. Your lower back remains in contact with the floor during a crunch. The crunch motion does not involve movement at the hips, unlike the sit-up. You can increase the difficulty of a crunch by placing your hands behind your head or elevating your legs on a bench.
summary: Variations and modifications for crunch exercises.

content: The Machine Rope Crunch targets the rectus abdominis, external oblique, and internal oblique. The Machine Rope Crunch can be performed facing either toward or away from the weight stack. The higher the hand position during the Machine Rope Crunch, the greater the difficulty. The torso should move from the upright position to almost parallel to the floor during the Machine Rope Crunch. The Machine Rope Crunch can be performed with a greater range of motion if you position yourself a short distance away from the pulley. Resistance during the Machine Rope Crunch can be altered by adjusting the weight stack.
summary: Description of the Machine Rope Crunch exercise.

content: The Machine Crunch targets the rectus abdominis and external oblique. To perform the Machine Crunch, sit in the seat of the machine, grasp the handles, and place your feet under the ankle pads. The torso should move from the upright position to almost parallel to the floor during the Machine Crunch.
summary: Description of the Machine Crunch exercise.

content: The Machine Crunch With Chest Pad targets the rectus abdominis, external oblique, and internal oblique. The hand position during the Machine Crunch With Chest Pad depends on the machine's design. The foot position during the Machine Crunch With Chest Pad depends on the machine's design. The body position during the Machine Crunch With Chest Pad depends on the machine's design. The torso should move from the upright position to almost parallel to the floor during the Machine Crunch With Chest Pad. Resistance during the Machine Crunch With Chest Pad depends on the machine's design.
summary: Description of the Machine Crunch With Chest Pad exercise.

content: The Seated Sit-Up targets the rectus abdominis, external oblique, and internal oblique. Secondary muscles involved in the Seated Sit-Up include the quadriceps (rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, vastus intermedius), and hip flexors (sartorius, iliopsoas). The starting position for the Seated Sit-Up is sitting on the apparatus with knees bent 90 degrees. Secure your feet under a roller pad or comparable support during the Seated Sit-Up. You may hold your hands together behind your lower back, cross them in front of your chest, or interlock them behind your head during the Seated Sit-Up.
summary: Description of the Seated Sit-Up exercise.

content: The Seated Sit-Up targets the rectus abdominis, external oblique, and internal oblique. Secondary muscles involved in the Seated Sit-Up include the quadriceps (rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, vastus intermedius), and hip flexors (sartorius, iliopsoas). The starting position for the Seated Sit-Up is sitting on the apparatus with knees bent 90 degrees. Secure your feet under a roller pad or comparable support during the Seated Sit-Up. You may hold your hands together behind your lower back, cross them in front of your chest, or interlock them behind your head during the Seated Sit-Up. The relative resistance in the Seated Sit-Up increases as your hands shift from your lower back to your chest to your head. Your torso should be close to vertically upright in the starting position and should lower back approximately 60 to 90 degrees during the Seated Sit-Up. Tension is released from the abdominal muscles when you lean too far back or bend too far forward during the Seated Sit-Up.
summary: The Seated Sit-Up primarily targets the core muscles, with secondary involvement from the quadriceps and hip flexors. Hand placement affects resistance.

content: You can add resistance to the Seated Sit-Up by holding a weight plate to your chest. The Twisting Seated Sit-Up involves adding a twisting motion during the sit-up. During the Twisting Seated Sit-Up, as you sit up, twist your torso, directing your right elbow toward your left knee. Lower your back to the starting position after twisting your torso during the Twisting Seated Sit-Up. During the next repetition of the Twisting Seated Sit-Up, direct your left elbow toward your right knee.
summary: Variations of the Seated Sit-Up include adding resistance and incorporating a twisting motion.

content: The Incline Leg Raise targets the rectus femoris, lower rectus abdominis, and external oblique. For the Incline Leg Raise, lie supine (face up) on an incline abdominal bench with your legs down, hands holding onto the bench above and behind your head.
summary: The Incline Leg Raise targets the lower abs and rectus femoris, performed on an incline bench.

content: The lower AbdominAls variation targets the lower rectus abdominis. Secondary muscles involved in the lower AbdominAls variation include the external oblique, internal oblique, iliopsoas, and rectus femoris. In the lower AbdominAls variation, your hands stabilize your torso by grasping the bench or handles above your head. In the lower AbdominAls variation, keep your feet together and your knees slightly bent. In the lower AbdominAls variation, your upper torso should remain in contact with the bench. To maximize contraction in the lower abdominals during the lower AbdominAls variation, lift your pelvis off the bench slightly as you raise your legs. To maximize muscle contraction on the way up during the lower AbdominAls variation, raise your knees as high as possible toward your chest. To keep tension on the abs during the lower AbdominAls variation, do not lower your legs all the way down or allow your feet to touch the floor.
summary: The lower AbdominAls variation specifically targets the lower rectus abdominis, with specific form cues for optimal contraction.

content: The angle of the bench to the floor affects the degree of difficulty in the lower AbdominAls variation. Tilting the bench at a steeper angle makes the lower AbdominAls variation more difficult. Reduce resistance in the lower AbdominAls variation by lowering the bench and decreasing the incline. Increase resistance in the lower AbdominAls variation by raising the bench and increasing the incline.
summary: The incline angle of the bench in the lower AbdominAls variation affects the exercise's difficulty and resistance.

content: For added resistance during the Dumbbell Incline Leg Raise, perform the exercise while holding a dumbbell between your feet.
summary: Adding a dumbbell between the feet increases resistance during the Dumbbell Incline Leg Raise.

content: To perform the Hanging Leg Raise, grasp a chin-up bar with your hands or place your elbows in a pair of ab slings. Let your legs hang down during the Hanging Leg Raise. Lift both knees, together and slightly bent, toward your chest during the Hanging Leg Raise. Slowly lower your legs to the starting position without swinging during the Hanging Leg Raise.
summary: The Hanging Leg Raise targets the rectus abdominis and is performed using a chin-up bar or ab slings.

content: The Hanging Leg Raise targets the rectus abdominis. Secondary muscles involved in the Hanging Leg Raise include the external oblique, internal oblique, and iliopsoas. For the Hanging Leg Raise, take a shoulder-width overhand grip on the chin-up bar and hang with your arms straight. Alternatively, use a pair of supportive upper-arm sleeves, like the ab sling device, for the Hanging Leg Raise. Keep your feet together and your knees slightly bent during the Hanging Leg Raise. Your torso should hang vertically, perpendicular to the floor during the Hanging Leg Raise. Raise your knees as high as possible to maximize muscular effort during the Hanging Leg Raise. As you lower your legs during the Hanging Leg Raise, keep your knees slightly bent to maintain tension on the abdominals.
summary: The Hanging Leg Raise primarily targets the rectus abdominis, with secondary involvement from the obliques and iliopsoas. Proper form and grip are crucial.

content: The Hanging Leg Raise is an exercise that targets the lower abdominal muscles. Your torso should hang vertically, perpendicular to the floor during the Hanging Leg Raise. Keep your feet together and your knees slightly bent during the Hanging Leg Raise. Raise your knees as high as possible to maximize muscular effort during the Hanging Leg Raise. As you lower your legs during the Hanging Leg Raise, keep your knees slightly bent to maintain tension on the abdominals. As you raise your legs during the Hanging Leg Raise, lift your pelvis to maximize contraction in the lower abdominals. This exercise is harder if you try to keep your legs straight during the Hanging Leg Raise. The more you bend your knees, the easier the Hanging Leg Raise becomes. Alternatively, use a pair of supportive upper-arm sleeves, like the ab sling device, for the Hanging Leg Raise.
summary: The Hanging Leg Raise targets the lower abs, emphasizing proper form and knee bend adjustments for difficulty.

content: The Knee-Up exercise targets the lower abdominal muscles. The primary muscles involved in the Knee-Up exercise are the rectus abdominis. Secondary muscles involved in the Knee-Up exercise include the external oblique, internal oblique, iliopsoas, and rectus femoris. To perform the Knee-Up exercise, sit on the edge of a flat, secure bench with legs hanging down and knees slightly bent. Grip the bench behind you for support during the Knee-Up exercise. Raise your knees toward your chest, keeping your legs together during the Knee-Up exercise. Lower your legs until your heels almost touch the floor during the Knee-Up exercise.
summary: The Knee-Up exercise primarily targets the rectus abdominis, with secondary involvement from obliques, hip flexors, and rectus femoris.

content: The Reverse Crunch exercise targets the lower abdominal muscles. The primary muscles involved in the Reverse Crunch exercise are the rectus abdominis. Secondary muscles involved in the Reverse Crunch exercise include the rectus femoris and external oblique. To perform the Reverse Crunch exercise, lie on a flat, secure bench with your feet positioned so that your knees and hips are bent 90 degrees. Grasp the bench behind your head for support during the Reverse Crunch exercise. Lift your pelvis off the bench until your feet point to the ceiling during the Reverse Crunch exercise. Lower your legs to the starting position during the Reverse Crunch exercise.
summary: The Reverse Crunch primarily targets the rectus abdominis, with assistance from the rectus femoris and external oblique.

content: The primary muscle involved in the variation is the rectus abdominis. Secondary muscles involved in the variation include the external oblique, internal oblique, and hip flexors (iliopsoas, rectus femoris). In the starting position for the variation, your thighs should be vertical and your lower legs should be parallel to the bench, resulting in a 90-degree bend at your knees and hips. Keep your feet and legs together during the variation. Keep your upper torso in contact with the bench during the variation. To perform the variation, contract your lower abs to lift your pelvis off the bench, raising your legs until your toes point to the ceiling. If you have difficulty balancing on a bench, you can perform the variation while lying on the floor.
summary: This variation primarily targets the rectus abdominis, with secondary involvement from obliques and hip flexors.

content: On hip flexor machines, resistance is provided by the machine and transmitted via a strap across your lower thighs. To perform the straight-leg sit-up, lie flat on the floor with your legs straight, feet secured, and arms at your sides. Keeping your back straight, raise your torso off the floor until your hands touch your knees during the straight-leg sit-up. Lower your torso to the starting position after raising it during the straight-leg sit-up.
summary: The straight-leg sit-up targets the lower rectus abdominis, emphasizing a straight back and controlled movement.

content: The primary muscle involved in the straight-leg sit-up is the lower rectus abdominis. Secondary muscles involved in the straight-leg sit-up include the external oblique, internal oblique, upper rectus abdominis, and hip flexors (sartorius, iliopsoas, rectus femoris. To perform the straight-leg sit-up, raise your torso off of the floor, keeping your spine straight. Your shoulders should rise about 6 to 12 inches (15-30 cm) off the floor or until your hands touch your knees during the straight-leg sit-up. The motion occurs at the waist and hips during the straight-leg sit-up, in contrast to crunches, where movement occurs at the upper spine.
summary: The straight-leg sit-up primarily targets the lower rectus abdominis, with secondary involvement from obliques, upper rectus abdominis, and hip flexors.

content: On the vertical leg raise apparatus, your back is supported against a backrest and your elbows rest on pads. This prevents your legs and torso from swinging during the Vertical Leg Raise.
summary: The Vertical Leg Raise utilizes a specialized apparatus to support the back and prevent swinging, isolating the leg raise movement.

content: The primary muscle involved in the straight-leg sit-up is the lower rectus abdominis. Secondary muscles involved in the straight-leg sit-up include the external oblique, internal oblique, upper rectus abdominis, and hip flexors (sartorius, iliopsoas, rectus femoris). To perform the straight-leg sit-up, raise your torso off of the floor, keeping your spine straight. Your shoulders should rise about 6 to 12 inches (15-30 cm) off the floor or until your hands touch your knees during the straight-leg sit-up. The motion occurs at the waist and hips during the straight-leg sit-up, in contrast to crunches, where movement occurs at the upper spine. Keep your legs straight during the straight-leg sit-up. The backs of your knees and your heels should be in contact with the floor throughout the movement during the straight-leg sit-up. Secure your feet under a roller pad or comparable support during the straight-leg sit-up. As an alternative, have a partner hold your ankles during the straight-leg sit-up. Keeping your arms out straight, your hands should slide along your thighs during the straight-leg sit-up. Terminating the motion when your hands touch your knees keeps focus on the lower abdominal muscles during the straight-leg sit-up. You can increase the degree of difficulty by placing your hands behind your head or by holding a weight to your chest during the straight-leg sit-up.
summary: The straight-leg sit-up is an exercise targeting the lower abdominal muscles, with proper form emphasizing a straight spine and controlled movement.

content: The Twisting Sit-Up exercise involves sitting on a decline bench, hooking your feet under the pad, leaning back, and positioning your hands behind your head. During a Twisting Sit-Up, as you sit up, twist your torso, directing your right elbow toward your left knee. After lowering back down to the starting position, direct your left elbow toward your right knee during the next repetition of a Twisting Sit-Up. The primary muscles involved in the Twisting Sit-Up are the rectus abdominis, external oblique, and internal oblique. Secondary muscles involved in the Twisting Sit-Up are the serratus anterior, sartorius, iliopsoas, and rectus femoris. For the Twisting Sit-Up, position your hands behind your head and secure your feet under a roller pad or comparable support. Keep your knees bent during a Twisting Sit-Up to reduce stress on the lower back. In the top position of a Twisting Sit-Up, your torso should be vertically upright with one elbow almost touching the opposite knee. Lower your torso until almost parallel to the floor, about three-quarters of the way down during a Twisting Sit-Up. Tilting the bench at a steeper angle makes the Twisting Sit-Up harder. Increase resistance in the Twisting Sit-Up by tilting the bench at a steeper incline or holding a small weight plate behind your head.
summary: The Twisting Sit-Up is a core exercise that targets the obliques and rectus abdominis, with variations in bench angle and added weight increasing difficulty.

content: The Broomstick Twist exercise involves sitting upright on the edge of a flat bench and holding a broomstick behind your neck. Twist your upper body from side to side during the Broomstick Twist exercise. When twisting to the right during the Broomstick Twist, feel the right oblique muscles contract.
summary: The Broomstick Twist is a simple exercise targeting the obliques, performed by twisting the upper body while holding a broomstick.

content: The Oblique Crunch exercise involves lying on your left side with your knees bent and together and your right hand behind your head. Slowly lift your upper body by contracting the obliques on your right side during the Oblique Crunch. Lower your torso back down after lifting it during the Oblique Crunch. The primary muscles involved in the Oblique Crunch are the external oblique, internal oblique, and rectus abdominis. Secondary muscles involved in the Oblique Crunch are the serratus anterior.
summary: The Oblique Crunch is a unilateral exercise targeting the obliques and rectus abdominis, performed by lifting the torso while contracting the obliques on one side.

content: The incline oblique crunch is performed using an incline abdominal chair. To perform the incline oblique crunch, secure your feet on the support platform and lean back sideways into the seat, resting on one buttock only. Place your uppermost hand behind your head and crunch your torso upward to perform the incline oblique crunch.
summary: The incline oblique crunch is a variation performed on an incline abdominal chair, targeting the obliques and rectus abdominis.

content: The machine oblique crunch is performed while sitting obliquly (on one buttock at a time) in the seat of a crunch machine, working one side at a time. The cable oblique crunch is performed by grabbing a D-handle attached to the high pulley of a cable machine. Crunch downward, directing your elbow toward the opposite knee during the cable oblique crunch. Slowly return to the starting position after crunching downward during the cable oblique crunch. The primary muscles involved in the cable oblique crunch are the external oblique, internal oblique, and serratus anterior. The secondary muscle involved in the cable oblique crunch is the rectus abdominis.
summary: The machine and cable oblique crunches are variations targeting the obliques and rectus abdominis, utilizing gym equipment for resistance.

content: The cable oblique crunch is performed by grabbing a D-handle attached to the high pulley of a cable machine. You may grasp the handle above or alongside your head during the cable oblique crunch. You may perform the cable oblique crunch while standing, kneeling, or sitting. You may perform the cable oblique crunch facing either toward or away from the weight stack. Crunch downward, directing your elbow toward the opposite knee during the cable oblique crunch. Slowly return to the starting position after crunching downward during the cable oblique crunch. Your torso should move from the upright position to almost parallel to the floor during the cable oblique crunch. You can alter the resistance of the cable oblique crunch by adjusting the weight stack.
summary: The cable oblique crunch is a versatile exercise targeting the obliques, with variations in grip, stance, and resistance.

content: To perform the cable standing oblique crunch, stand side-on to the weight stack and grab the D-handle attached to a high pulley with your nearside hand. Crunch downward, directing your elbow to the hip on the same side during the cable standing oblique crunch.
summary: The cable standing oblique crunch is a specific variation targeting the obliques.

content: The rope oblique crunch involves holding the rope attachment with both hands and crunching with a twist to one side and then the other. The rope oblique crunch motion is similar to that used during twisting sit-ups.
summary: The rope oblique crunch is a twisting variation targeting the obliques.

content: The primary muscles involved in the cable oblique crunch are the external oblique, internal oblique, and serratus anterior. The secondary muscle involved in the cable oblique crunch is the rectus abdominis.
summary: The cable oblique crunch primarily targets the obliques and secondarily the rectus abdominis.

content: The Dumbbell Side Bend exercise targets the external oblique, internal oblique, and serratus anterior muscles. Secondary muscles involved in the Dumbbell Side Bend include the rectus abdominis and quadratus lumborum. Hold a dumbbell at arm’s length by your side in one hand and place the other hand behind your head. Stand with your feet hip-width apart for the Dumbbell Side Bend. When bending to the right side during the Dumbbell Side Bend, you work the left obliques, and vice versa. Your torso should bend approximately 45 degrees or until the dumbbell becomes level with your knee during the Dumbbell Side Bend. The torso should move directly sideways without leaning to the front or the back during the Dumbbell Side Bend. Avoid using a heavy dumbbell for the Dumbbell Side Bend.
summary: The Dumbbell Side Bend targets the obliques and other core muscles, emphasizing controlled sideways bending.

content: The Cable Side Bend exercise targets the external oblique, internal oblique, serratus anterior, and rectus abdominis muscles. The obliques muscles involved are the primary external oblique, internal oblique, and serratus anterior. Secondary obliques muscles involved are the rectus abdominis and quadratus lumborum. For the oblique exercise, hold the D-handle attached to the low pulley at arm’s length in one hand and place your other hand behind your head. Stand with your feet slightly wider than your hips for the oblique exercise. The position of your legs and arms should resemble a four-pointed star for the oblique exercise. Stand sufficiently away from the pulley so that the gripping arm remains outstretched for the oblique exercise. When you hold the weight in your right hand you work the left oblique muscles, and vice versa for the oblique exercise. Your torso should bend sideways back and forth through approximately 60 degrees, or between 10 and 2 o’clock when looking in a mirror for the oblique exercise. You may bend toward the weight stack to stretch the oblique before contracting the muscle in the opposite direction for the oblique exercise. Your torso should move directly sideways without leaning forward or backward for the oblique exercise. Adjust the resistance on the weight stack according to your ability for the oblique exercise. Use caution with heavier weights, as overdeveloped oblique muscles will make your waist appear bulky for the oblique exercise.
summary: The Cable Side Bend is a weighted exercise targeting the obliques and other core muscles, emphasizing controlled sideways bending.

content: For the dumbbell pullover, lie with your upper back resting across a flat bench. Hold a dumbbell straight above your chest. Lower the dumbbell down and back until it reaches bench level, inhaling deeply and stretching your rib cage as you do so. Pull the weight back up to the vertical position, exhaling as you do so.
summary: The dumbbell pullover is a chest and back exercise that involves controlled lowering and raising of a dumbbell.

content: The dumbbell pullover is performed by lying with your upper back resting across a flat bench. Grip the dumbbell by placing your palms against the inside of the weight plate at one end, making a diamond shape around the bar with your thumbs and index fingers. Your torso should remain still and parallel to the floor, with your upper back resting on the bench and your feet firm on the floor for stability. Hold a dumbbell straight above your chest. Lower the dumbbell down and back until it reaches bench level, inhaling deeply and stretching your rib cage as you do so. Pull the weight back up to the vertical position, exhaling as you do so. Move the dumbbell through an arc of about 90 degrees. Aim for a full stretch in your rib cage as you lower the weight. Do not use a heavy weight because the shoulder joint is vulnerable to injury during this exercise.
summary: Instructions for performing the dumbbell pullover exercise, emphasizing proper form and safety.

content: Primary muscles involved in the dumbbell pullover are the serratus anterior, intercostals, and latissimus dorsi. Secondary muscles involved in the dumbbell pullover are the pectoralis major, pectoralis minor, and triceps brachii.
summary: Muscles targeted during the dumbbell pullover exercise, categorized as primary and secondary.

content: This exercise strengthens the serratus anterior but doesn’t directly target the obliques. However, it is included in this section because the serratus anterior is worked during most oblique exercises. Use caution with heavier weights, as overdeveloped oblique muscles will make your waist appear bulky for the oblique exercise.
summary: Relationship between the dumbbell pullover and oblique exercises, noting potential for waist appearance changes with heavy weights.
